top_profane.text:
Dirty Bastard(Grim Bastards 1)
Wow!!!! Trix and Boz are amazing and hot!!! I absolutely loved this book from page 1. Trix is the daughter of different clubs president. Boz is the president of the Grim Bastards. Boz wants her for himself and what Boz wants, he most definitely will get. The chemistry that these two have is electric!
I can not wait to read the rest of this series!!!!

Filthy Bastard(Grim Bastards 2)
We first met Brew and Addy in Dirty Bastard, I loved how they were together from the start. He calmed her from the beginning. I didn't think I would like this book as much but I loved it more..... The past that would have torn other people made them better together. It had everything I look for in a great book.... Laughs, tears, happy and sad. And let's not forget really good sex!!!!

I will definitely be recommending this book to all of my friends!!!!! And I look forward to the rest of the series!!!!!!!

Guilty Bastard(Grim Bastards 3)
At first I wasn't sure whether I wanted to punch Round or beat him.... My heart broke for Lisa. But as the story went on, you couldn't help fall in love with them both. I CAN NOT wait for Nasty to come out!!!! Another winner!!

Nasty Bastard(Grim Bastards 4)
Nasty Bastard by Emily Minton and Shelley Springfield is the 4th book in the Grim Bastards MC Series. How is it that they just keep getting better and better. This is most definitely a 5 star book in my opinion.

This is Smoke and Gidgets book. They were highschool sweethearts. Each other's first everything. They were both born into this life. Smoke knew that he was born to wear a Grim Bastards cut and have Gidget as his old lady. That is until his father get it in his head that they are too young to be talking marriage and the rest of their lives. Gidget leaves for college and they both move on but never truly forgetting each other. After her father and brother “disappear” and she ends up divorced, she moves back home with her son. Is their love strong enough to pick up where they left off all those years ago? Can Gidget put her trust in smoke that he won't push her away again? True love will win in the end!!

Wicked Bastard(Grim Bastards5)
Wicked Bastard, Grim Bastards MC by Emily Minton and Shelley Springfield

I can not tell you how long I have been waiting for this book.
When Hack takes a leave after losing his wife, he stops in a diner and meets Pru there. Spending everyday for 3 months together, they are practically inseparable, until one day, Hack just ups and leaves. For months after leaving, he gets numerous texts and calls but never checks what the messages are. One day, missing her like crazy, he opens up the last text she sent him and is shocked to find out that he left her with a very special gift. A baby. Can Hack win Pru back be there for his daughter?

Death of a Bastard
This book gutted me in the first few pages. I had to put it down because I couldn't see through the tears. This book truly broke my heart but also made me eager to see what is going to happen with the next generation of the Grim Bastards. I am not going to mention and names and give anything away but I will say that even tho I cried like a baby, I was glad that it ended the way that it did. I can not wait to see what you have in story for us next!!!

top_profane.title: An amazing series all in one box set!!!!

top_profane.text:
Damned good read. Spoiler alert.

This one starts with Dick waking up at the farm the Jamie and her Sheriff Husband Steve own. Many of Steve's deputies and their families also live on this farm that is surrounded by growing corn and other crops.

Doc tells Dick he's going through withdrawal from the drugs his captors hooked him on. This isn't the first time Dick will be going through withdrawal but he hopes its the last.

The withdrawal works and though Dick still can't remember all of his past he does get some of it back.

Courtney is still with him and her boyfriend, soon to be husband, Louie was killed rescuing a wounded Steve. FEMA and the NSA attacked the farm looking for Dick and the runaways which are what the Deputies and their families are. Dick can see the fixed bullet holes and broken glass from the attack which occurred when he was unconscious. Dick figures they will be back.

It takes Dick a while to get moving but Steve wants his help to set up protections for the farm. Steve also tells him that once he's done he wants him gone. He knows Dick has feelings for his wife and daughter. Dick totally understands and he's still anxious to get to Arkansas to see Mary and Maggie. Dick also knows the deputies resent him and one of them takes a swing at him. He quickly puts the idiot on the ground and now knows there will be hard feelings from all of the deputies.

He, Doc Soames and Courtney are checking out the farm when they see vehicles at the front gate. Once Dick gets a good look the men he knows something isn't right. These guys are to clean and after listening to their speech he detects an accent. Very faint, but an accent none the less. Dick has a Masters degree in History and spent seventeen years in Special Forces and he knows a rat when he smells on.

Wright is Steve's number two and he's telling these guys to leave. They keep saying they only want to spend the night. Dick opens the gate, pulls his gun and points it into the leaders face giving his three seconds to drop weapons. Dick ends up shooting two of them before the others get the idea. He is then shot from behind and the men in front of him open up on those at the farm. Dick had on his vest with plates but getting shot still hurts like hell. The SAW the men in front of him had also left three bullet holes in the front of that vest. The vest that saved his life.

Doc Soames who was with Dick and Courtney shot and killed the deputy that shot Dick. The same Deputy Dick had put on the ground earlier. The men in front of them turned out to be UN Troops and FEMA. The survivors left in the end truck. The farm lost Wright and a few others. Dick knows the survivors will be back.

So begins one damned fine read.

This one has Dick, Courtney, Jamie, Mel, Steve, the deputies that despise and attack Dick, Doc Soames, Mary, Maggie, a kid named Ricky and his family that Dick and Courtney help, the family that killed Ricky's dad and took his mother, sister and all the supplies they had in the house, a Dick and Courtney who kill the men, get Ricky's mom and sister back, help one of the wives with a sick baby, a wife who will be staying with Ricky and his family, a Dick and Courtney who finally make it to Arkansas, a Dick who finally see's Mary, Maggie who absolutely loves Dick, Mary's Father who doesn't care much for Dick, a Dick who finds out he's not who he thinks he is, a Mary who has a story to tell and Dick Parish who is doing his best to understand his past, help those he can and stay alive.

Five Stars. Super read.
top_profane.title: Two thumbs up, way up for this one.

top_profane.text:
Damned fine read. Spoiler alert.

This one starts with Dick, Jamie, Mel, Courtney and Luis heading out from Chicago for Arkansas so Dick can find his ex wife and daughter. Jamie and Mel will be headed for their own home and Jamie's husband.

They have bikes, three, so they have to double up on two of them so everyone can ride.

Dick knows its not going to be easy but the others just aren't savvy enough to know just how hard it can be. Dick is constantly on them about noise discipline and situational awareness.

Dick Pershing is and ex soldier who served many deployments in the sand box. He was one helluva soldier but his career cost him his wife. She divorced him just before he got out after a twenty two year career.

Pershing fell into drugs and homelessness. He was living in the underground of Chicago when the EMP hit. He survived and rescued several kids which he cared for. Dick is still one helluva soldier and a damned good tactician. A man who despite everything is very capable.

The group travels at night using NVG's and sleeps during the day. Things are going fine and the bikes are better than walking. They are making pretty good time. The do see some people, and do their best not to be seen. They all know there are people out there who will kill you for what you have.

Just beyond the town of Aurora they are chased by a pack of dogs. Dogs that are trained and used by men. Dick takes over. He sends the others on their way on the bikes while he kills the dogs and takes on the men. Seems there were just two. A man and his son. Dick wounds the man after the man tells his son to go back for the rest of the group.

The man is badly wounded and Dick asks him if he wants Dick to kill him. He's been gut shot and his intestines are all over. Dick shoots him in the head and takes down the vehicle when it shows up. Killing everyone in the vehicle except for the mans son. A son Dick promised the Father he wouldn't kill. A promise that will bit Dick in the ass.

Dick heads out to meet his crew and it takes him two days on foot before he finds Mel. Seems the others have been taken by a group. Dick, of course will be going hunting.

So begins one damned fine read.

This one has Dick, Jamie, her daughter Mel, Courtney, Luis, a community that's doing pretty good, a community that took them prisoner but doesn't hurt them, they have a doctor and a Pastor who seems to be in charge, a community with a big problem, the problem being a group of men who demand payment so these men won't hurt anyone, a pastor who asks Dick for help, a Dick who sets it up, a community who grow a set of balls and take back their town, a Dick and crew who are captured by men in hummers, a Dick who is rehooked on drugs, a Dick that the Dr. Skinner the the man in charge thinks is someone named Norton, a Dick who knows Norton and a Navy SEAL, a man Dick worked with a few times in the sandbox, a Norton who is a very good man, a Dick who is tortured in an underground bunker, a bunker that comes under attack, a Dick who kills his guards and his torturer Skinner, Jamie and her husband Steve who are part of the rescue and Dick Pershing doing his best to get to his ex wife and daughter, help those he can and stay alive.

Five Stars
top_profane.title: Thumbs up

top_profane.text:
Full disclosure: having contributed a story to Doctors in Hell, I do not feel right about reviewing this 18h volume in the classic Heroes in Hell shared-universe series. And Amazon won't let me post this without giving the book some kind of star rating. So forgive me if I give it a good rating, because the writers assembled for this volume are top-notch, and they deserve the 5-star rating. I am humble and yet honored to be a part of this edition. If I may, I'd like to tell you a little about this 2015 volume in the saga of Heroes in Hell, and my part in this grand project.

The series is often called “Bangsian fantasy,” a genre of fantasy which concerns the use of famous literary or historical individuals and their interactions in the afterlife. It’s named for John Kendrick Bangs (1862 –1922), an American satirist who often wrote such tales. Heroes in Hell itself is an epic series of shared-world novels where the famous and infamous throughout history all wind up together in Hell, where they virtually pick up right where they left off when still alive — but now with a diabolical twist: Hell may give you what you want and what you need, but these things are never quite what you asked for. Hell is not what you’d expect, so always expect the unexpected. Things are broken in Hell, things malfunction, and there’s always a grand touch of irony to everything that happens. Hell gives and Hell takes away, and in Hell the Damned get just what they deserve. There is comedy and tragedy in this eternal and infernal arena of Lost Souls, where human drama is played out across a wide spectrum of such literary genres that include heroic fantasy, horror, action-adventure, political thrillers, westerns, science fiction, and even romance. Each individual story in each book reads like a chapter in a novel, and each story/chapter bears the unique touch and personality of its author.

The premise of the series is based on the tradition that 613 is the number of mitzvoth or commandments in the Torah, which began in the 3rd century CE when Rabbi Simlai mentioned it in a sermon that is recorded in Talmud Makkot 23b. Our series of novels begins with the 613 original commandments, binding on every living soul, and ignorance is no excuse: break just one little commandment and you go to Hell. So almost everybody who was anybody broke some commandment or other while on earth, and now here they are, sometimes in a part of Hell where they belong, sometimes in an area of Hell where they don’t. The Damned come from across the length and breadth of time and history to interact, to scheme and plot, and even go adventuring — all the while suffering the torments of a well-deserved damnation. The worst and best from all of time make the same mistakes in Hell that got them there in the first place: character is destiny, in life Topside and in the Afterlife of the underverse, as well. You could read these books in order, in any order, or without having read any of the previous volumes in the series. In Hell, Time is meaningless, so it doesn't matter which book you begin with: start anywhere, for the cohesion in each volume makes it stand alone. You can read Hell forward or backward or upside down: Hell is still Hell. It still unsettles minds and makes hearts skip beats. The Damned get the Hell they deserve. Expect what will be, nothing less, and nothing more. This is not your mommy’s world of fantasy: this is Hell, and tonight we dine on gore, tonight we feast on souls.

For all the horrors and torments that Satan has unleashed upon the Damned, the Almighty has decided that he’s been too lenient on them, and so to Hell were sent Erra, the Babylonian plague god, and his henchmen, the Seven Sibitti, to spread plague and terror, to wreak havoc and further punishment throughout the underverse. Erra then stirs the pit by adding his own little brand of mayhem, maleficence, and malefic maladies to the mix. The result is that pestilential misery runs amok in Hell, lost souls wail in even more torment, doctors raise their fees, and snake-oil salesmen make a killing selling all sorts of bootlegged versions of vaccines and so-called remedies for the plagues sweeping across and through all levels and circles of Hell. But the damned must suffer, and the Devil is furious about Erra and his enforcers being sent from Heaven to prove that Hell is insufficiently hellish. And since death in Hell for all lost souls is only fleeting, followed by a horrifying turn in the Mortuary where they are worked on by the Undertaker prior to being reassigned, torment and suffering are eternal.

There is no escaping Hell. And don’t bother telling Hell’s doctors where it hurts, they won’t care. They have their own problems. Ah, but Satan has a plan. Satan always has a plan. It’s a purge that may be even more terrible than anything cooked up by Erra. Satan, you see, has always held to the belief that Mankind is worthy of neither salvation nor damnation, and deserves only oblivion: total obliteration into nothingness. His Satanic Majesty has been trying to prove his point to Heaven and the Big Man Upstairs for ages upon ages, and this argument is what landed him in Hell in the first place. The Devil has always insisted that modern souls in Hell — called the New Dead, roughly anyone born Anno Domini — are so vicious, self-centered, hubristic and morally bankrupt that they would punish themselves and each other, if given a chance, more horribly and thoroughly than Hell’s bureaucracy could ever contrive to do. This leads to a bet between Satan and the angel Altos, who wants to prove the New Dead worthy of salvation — or at least deserving of leniency, to show themselves no worse than their predecessors or successors.

This brings us to the first story, "The Wager," by Janet Morris and Chris Morris, wherein Altos, Hell’s only volunteer angel, has been sent from Above to effect Satan’s rehabilitation, a daunting task. Altos and Satan wager on the outcome of a battle between 20th and 21st century militarists who, Satan says, “will combat one another in battles fought exclusively by volunteers: armies manned by voyeurs of violence who find vicarious thrills reading of heroes who never were, fighting villains who never could be. If we hold this war and nobody comes, or the doctors of the damned heal the wounded and save the plague-ridden, then, Altos, you will win, and I shall soften my heart unto the New Dead and forestall the purge you know I am readying.”

Chris Morris follows this up with "The Cure," where Satan orders John Milton: “Tell Marlowe you have learned the difference between oblivion, impossible in my domain, and obliteration, which a soul can claim, be he brave enough: obliteration — complete and sweet: Not only ‘not to be,’ but to be expunged as if he’d never been at all. This will make an end to his playwriting and poetry, and an end to his affair with Shakespeare.” So Milton, horrified at what he hears next, must infect Christopher Marlowe with the knowledge of this cure.

Next up is Andrew Paul Weston’s tale, "Grim." Satan demands a purging laxative to clear the bowels of the underworld of the dross that has accumulated over the centuries, and turns to the doctors for assistance. However, it appears our infernal physicians are hell-bent on fomenting rebellion. Forced to act, His Satanic Majesty turns to his Chief of Surgical Strikes and cure-all remedy — Daemon Grim — to wield the scalpel of injustice . . . and wield it he does.

In "The Right Man for the Job," Deborah Koren’s story, we learn that the only thing worse than having Wyatt Earp gunning for you is having Wyatt Earp and plague victims after you. Bat Masterson joins forces with Dr. Henry Porter, the only surviving surgeon from the Little Big Horn, in order to stay alive.

The main premise of Nancy Asire’s "Memory" is the plague that’s struck hell and Napoleon’s memories of dealing with plague during his Egyptian campaign. The ramifications of these memories color his actions when dealing with the threat to those he cares about and shows the response of his friends in the face of potential disaster.

R.E. Hinkle’s story is "What Price Oblivion?" In this, he writes of 19th Century confidence man Charles “Doc” Baggs, who abhorred violence in life, but finds himself in death forced to be the thing he loathes the most, so much so that even oblivion is preferable to the monstrosity he has become. But when he encounters another doomed soul in worse torment than his own, who deserves that oblivion more than Baggs himself craves it, he finds himself tempted to take action. Can there be good deeds, even in Hell?

Richard Groller’s "In the Shadowlands" picks up where his previous story, (“Island Out of Time”) left off. Houdini’s brief escape from Hell results in him returning to Hell with an unwitting passenger: a living lawyer, not yet a member of the damnable dead. His self-assigned mission is now to return the lawyer to the land of the living before it is too late.

In Matthew Kirshenblatt’s "Let Us Kill the Spirit of Gravity," a fallen angel awaits and a Beast awakens as Lilith, the first wife of Adam, and the philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche come to an unlikely accord.

"Pavlovian Slip," by Bill Snider, is up next. In Hell, one would expect that psychologists would be in their place; the variety, the divergences of human experience, the interactions, the very grist of individual will and the exercise thereof. But, for Ivan Pavlov and Sigmund Freud, there can be no joy of discovery, there can only be the persistence of existence, in Hellish accord. When Ivan and his demonic horde of Grumbles join with Sigmund . . . what kinds of insanity are likely to happen?

My own story, "Hell on a Technicality," continues the misadventures of Doctor Victor Frankenstein who, with the assistance of Quasimodo, concocts a plague vaccine that has some unforeseen and diabolical side effects. Meanwhile, Galatea and Frankenstein’s Monster visit a panel of so-called experts to find out if they have or don’t have souls — and if they don’t, can they get out of Hell on a technicality?

In Michael H. Hanson’s "Convalescence," Nurse Calamity Jane, with the help of her Sinchester Rifle, protects Satan’s final outpost, The St. Rictus Nursing Home, from the all-encompassing plagues sweeping across Hell.

Paul Freeman’s "Hell Noon" deals with the plagues sweeping through hell, corrupting souls already suffering the harshest torments, and a group of gamblers holed up in a saloon on the outskirts of the Dead Plains. Doc Holliday leads the motley crew of damned souls as they seek to sit out the spreading contagion. But hell holds no place to hide from Satan’s punishments, least of all for a gambling man seeking to con the lord of all evil.

In "The Judas Book," by Jack William Finley, Lobotomist Dr. Walter Freeman thinks he’s got a loophole to free himself from Hell. Judas Iscariot thinks he’s got Hell’s news bestseller, and Frank Nitti thinks they are both a pain in his ass worthy of Hell.

Now we come to the end of it all with "Writer’s Block," by Janet Morris and Chris Morris. This time out, Shakespeare insists on taking Christopher Marlowe to the most infamous witch doctors in hell, where Marlowe begs their aid to find his lost Muse: “Can you help us? Spin a spell? Weave a charm? Vex a potion? Hex an enemy? Do any magics such as your sign outside boasts you can?”
“I can. I’ll give ye a push toward destiny,” cackles one bristly hag.
And the witch doctors do just that.

Oh, wait! We’re not quite finished yet. As a special treat, there’s "A Moment of Clarity," a wonderful excerpt from Andrew Paul Weston’s forthcoming novel, "Hell Bound."

So there we are, Doctors in Hell, where the doctor is always wrong, sinners never win, misery runs amok, and Hell’s damned get their just deserts . . . eternally. I hope you join our Company of 13 Hellions on a journey through all the pits, circles and levels of Hell, where not only doctors, but explorers, warriors, playwrights, lawyers, rogues, dreamers, and poets become an unlikely band of heroes — and anti-heroes — in Hell.
top_profane.title: A VIEW FROM INSIDE HELL

top_profane.text:
I have very mixed feelings about this series, first I don't like the way seeley writes Dick this is fanon Dick, this series hasn't felt like a rebirth at all, yeah Dick is in gotham and is back in blue but the reasons why I loved this character are for how kind hearted he is and how he puts on a smile when the other batfam members are brooding but Dick is also super smart and a good detective he also takes things seriously, the reason why Dick trusts raptor so much is because Dick thinks they are very much a like, my favorite character Dick Grayson would never just trust someone like raptor just because he sees how much they seem to have in common, he isn't that dumb, Dick has regressed so much and as a huge Dick Grayson fan this really upsetting, while everyone else in rebirth is getting to be as great as they were again pre n52, Dick being one of the few with his own series that just hasn't gotten that along with batgirl, you really want me to believe that this Dick has been Batman before and that Bruce actually thinks he did a good job considering that its been stated that once Bruce dies Dick is to take up the mantle, really now? he can't hold his own in a fight and he sure as hell does not act as if he's been Batman, this is so weird to me since on Twitter I've seem some dc writers say they want to make Dick as great as Batman, wonder woman.. you know one of the big guys, but that is not going to happen if they keep writing Dick like this, but apparently there's plans, moving on I'm was really confused about the owls stroyline 78% of the time, as much as I don't like Scott Snyder's Batman he at least did a great job where the court of owls are concerned and showed us how threatening and scary the court is but in nightwing they just don't seem all that scary, they felt like an afterthought, they pretty much have become a joke, though I'm glad that Dick isn't undercover anymore.

I love the way Seeley writes Dick and Damian relationship it's really cute, but when I read the scene between Dick and Bruce I wanted to slam my head against the wall, we're going back to Dick and Bruce having trust issues, I thought that Bruce Wayne was gone, Please god we've been over this stroyline with Dick and Bruce already so many times can we please just not, the pacing of the story feels slow.

Now to the positives, like I said i love the way Seeley writes Damian, and they way he writes Dick and Damians relationship, at times this series can be pretty fun, I love with tiger that ongoing tony joke, and laughed at Bruce telling Damian that Alfred didn't wet his, speaking of Alfred when is his relationship with Dick going to return, Dick was like a son to him and Alfred was like a father to Dick, is Alfred only allowed to appear in Batman now? With all that being said I'd rate this issue as a 6 or 7/10

Dirty Bastard(Grim Bastards MC #1)
✯✯5 Boz Sized Stars!!✯✯

Emily Minton just keeps killing it! This time she has a brand new co-author. Shelley Springfield. You honestly can't tell which author wrote what. I read this book in one sitting.

When Boz was 22yrs old he met the most adorable, funniest beautiful 14yr old. Trix, the daughter of the President of Satan's Revenge MC. Boz's father is the President of his MC the Grim Bastards. Boz doesn't agree with the way his dad is running the MC. But, meeting Trix is the only good thing lately. He can't wait to see her when she's of legal age.

Four years later and Trix is getting ready to leave for college. Her dad is throwing her a party and a few other MC's. All she cares about is seeing Boz. The guy who's starred in every fantasy and dream the past few years. When they see each other again the connection they felt all those years ago is still there and even stronger then they remembered. Just as Trix was about to give herself to Boz tragedy hit the party. Boz's life changed that night.

Years later Boz is the President of the Grim Bastards MC. His club has gotten into some new business. Everything has finally started to look up for the club. Until something important gets stolen from them. When Boz is told that Hoss the President of Satan's Revenge was behind it. He knows how he'll get his stuff back. Steal what means the most to Hoss. His daughter Trix.

Trix couldn't wait until she got to see Boz. But, she wasn't expecting that she'd see him because he had her and her best friend Addy kidnapped. When she sees him she thought she'd be mad and unattracted to him. But, nope Trix is still very much attracted to Boz.

The feelings are mutual between Boz and Trix. Anybody within 100ft of these two can see and feel the heat coming off them. Boz tells Trix exactly why she's there. Boz wants Trix as his. He plans on keeping her until she is. Trix wants Boz. Always has and always will.

I was hooked from the very first page. I've never wanted to get kidnapped before, but after reading Dirty Bastard, I'd love for Boz or any of the Grim Bastards to come and take me! That should tell you how awesome this was. MC books are some of my favorites to read. This one was no different. Just when I knew what was next or who was lying and playing games. My heart broke for Boz more then once. He's such an incredible guy. He's cares about his MC and loves Trix. Boz has always tried to do what he thinks is right. Then there's Trix. Love her!! She grew up living at the MC compound. She's strong, feisty, smart mouthed and still knows there's a time and place for everything. She's also the best friend to have. The way Trix loves, cares and protects Addy is the beautiful. They really are good people. But, together their great!!

Filthy Bastard(Grim Bastards MC #2)
✯✯5 Filthy Stars!!!✯✯

I knew when I finished Dirty Bastard that I needed the next book immediately. But, man I was so unprepared for what I was getting into. This book can definitely have some triggers for some people. I luckily don't have any.

We start off at the part where Brew & Crank kidnapped Trix & Addy and we go from there. Getting their POV of what happened great. Watching them fall for each other was even more great. Until Brew wants Addy to stay and she tells him no. It's obvious there's more to the story Addy is telling both Brew & Trix. Trix talks Brew into going after her and sees something he's not prepared for. It's not until Trix, Boz and Brew sit down to talk that Brew figures most of it out. And, it's his worst nightmare coming back. Luckily for him he has Boz and his brothers at his back. Because, the last time this happened his family didn't have his back and he spent years in juvie then prison.

Everyone who looks at Addy thinks she got the perfect life and the perfect family. But, it's far from perfect. Addy and her older sister Alex have been tortured and abused by their parents for as long as they can remember. If Alex didn't heart surgery the two of them would've been long gone. Now, their plans are once Alex delivers her son and gets her surgery they're running away. Until Brew stepped into Addy's life.

Brew's family threw him away that awful night and never looked back. But, an unlikely source sent him to the Grim Bastards. Now, he has a family. But, deep down Brew is missing something. When he laid his eyes on the tiny red head his life was never the same. But, when he found out what she'd been going thru he made it his mission to save her and show her what it's like to be loved and safe.

Brew Addy's knight wearing a shining MC cut. He had his demons. But, I feel his brothers in the MC rescued and saved him. Their conversations had me dying with laughter. Then, there were times when no words needed to be passed btwn them to know what was needed. It was great. The way he knew what Addy needed and how to deal with it, was beautiful.

Addy was extremely strong. The things she'd done to protect the one closest to her was both beautiful and stupid. I'm so glad Brew and the guys got there before something seriously happened. Addy was completely herself when she was kidnapped and around Brew. These two met under crazy circumstances. They lived different lifestyles. But, they come from the same life. They're absolutely beautiful together. And, man oh man are they also hot!! Phew! I had to fan myself off a bunch and then grab tissues the next.

Guilty Bastard (Grim Bastards MC #3)
✯✯4.5 Guilty Stars!!!✯✯

Wow!! I loved the first two books in this series and this was no different. It' a great quick read. Even though all three books are standalones I would definitely read them IN order.

This is Round's story. He's the old VP of the Grim Bastards. He stepped down when Baz's dad died and he took over. Round knew his son Smoke was the right man to sit at Baz's side to run the club the right way. Round's life definitely wasn't what he wanted. His life was his MC. He has proved it time and time again. Including when his daughter was against it. His Old Lady was everything but a proper Old Lady. Only in name. After she dies tragically he moves Lisa into his house. He's been in love with her for years now. Even though she'd be the perfect Old Lady and has been asked multiple times already. Round says he'll never take another Old Lady.

Lisa has been part of the Grim Bastards since she was seventeen. Baz's father gave her the job of over looking of the girls. When she fell in love with Round she stopped being a club girl.What his wife died and he moved her into his house she thought after six years he was going to finally make her his. But, after months of living out of a bag and with reminders of his wife all over the place. If that wasn't bad enough, Lisa hears words come out of Round's mouth that knocks the wind outta her lungs. Lisa went from feeling loved and at home, to a place that feels lonely and cold. She needs to do what's best for her before Round can open his mouth.

I absolutely loved this!! Lisa is just as awesome, strong, sexy and tough as the two previous ladies. Lisa has earned the respect from all the guys and ladies of this MC. She really looks out for the girls. But, that does not mean she can't tell when they're full of crap and call them out. And, in front of everyone! And, Round. I absolutely wanted to strangle him with my tiny hands. Multiple times! He definitely did not listen to his heart. It made me scream at times. I mean I understood his reasoning's behind his thinking. I do believe he should've acted differently. He's lucky he has Smoke. That's all I'm saying about that.

I love Emily and Shelley's co-writing. You never know who's writing what. Once I open their book it's not getting shut until it's done. That's how good they are.

Nasty Bastard (Grim Bastards MC #4)
✯✯5 Smokey Stars!!!✯✯

Man, do i love this series!! There's nothing about this series that I don't like. Well, maybe that I have to wait for the next book to release. I'm not a patient person lol. Emily & Shelley are the perfect writing duo.

Layton "Smoke" Roundtree and Gidget Etheridge have grown up together being that their fathers are both in the Grim Bastards MC. They're in love and are planning on getting married. Once they plan on getting married Gidget plans on going to community college for nursing and Layton joining the MC. Until one night Gidget is blindsided when Smoke breaks up with her and throws her out of the clubhouse.

They haven't seen or heard from each other since that night. Gidget had moved away to get her nursing degree, got married and had her son Parker. Now, she's divorced and it's just her and her son. With her father and brother missing, not that she's complaining. Gidget moves into the house she grew up in. It doesn't start off as welcoming to her as she runs into someone looking for her father. They take out their frustrations on Gidget and leave.

Smoke has only ever loved Gidget. It's why he's never taken an Old Lady of his own. It belongs to Gidget. When him, Boz and Hack head to her house they weren't expecting to find her there or beaten up badly. Smoke brings Gidget and Parker to the clubhouse to keep them safe.Smoke refuses to let Gidget slip from his hands again.

This was just as good as the previous books. This really was a second chance romance story. Gidget and Smoke really loved each other and are perfect for each other. Just like the men before Smoke, he's the perfect alpha male, loving and caring all while being nasty and filthy in bed. Gidget is such a strong woman. She's the perfect woman to sit at Smoke's side as his Old Lady.

Emily and Shelley are one of my favorite co-writers. You never know who's written what part. They have come up with an incredible MC storyline over the last 4 books. Just when I thought they couldn't surprise me. I read this and OMG I def wouldn't wanna get on their bad side.

Wicked Bastard (Grim Bastards MC #5)
✯✯5 Hack Size Stars!!!✯✯

The Grim Bastards are back and as ever.I been hooked since the very beginning of this series. Pru and Hack were no different.

Hack lost his wife. He ended up on a downward spiral into a dangerously ugly gutter. When his President told him to go and get straightened out, he left.But, when he walked into that dingy diner he was unprepared for what was next.

Pru was unprepared for her life to change after she waited on his table. Hack has invaded her life. She's use to her mothers abuse. She's been getting it her entire life. Hack treats her like a queen. Until the morning he dips out on her. Without a word except a stack of cash and a letter.

Hack left but didn't stop thinking about Pru. She didn't make it easy on him. She's in his mind and on his heart. She has been blowing up his phone. When Hack finally breaks down and listens to her last message his heart drops. Pru has given birth to his daughter.

Hack runs to go get his woman and daughter back. He's going to do whatever it takes to get her back. When Hack gets there he falls so much more in love with Pru and is a complete goner with his love for his daughter. But, then Hack notices something dangerous. Pru and his daughter are being watched. Hack and his brothers now have to find out why. Hack vows to keep his girls safe. No matter what!

I read this in 2 sittings. It was fast paced. This one was definitely a second chance at romance, forgiveness, love and life. Emily and Shelley are perfect together. I hope they keep going.

Death of a Bastard
✯✯5 Tearful Stars!!!✯✯

I won't say much about who this story is about. But, I will say it was the end of an incredible man. I cried from start to finish. And, to see how the kids have grown up. This was a definite ending to an incredible story.
top_profane.title: ✯✯5 MC Strong Stars!!!✯✯

top_profane.text:
Spoiler-free review:

The New 52 has de-aged Dick Grayson, and he is now back to his crime fighting as Nightwing. Even in the New 52 past, Dick still subbed in as Batman for a bit while Bruce Wayne was missing, and it seems like this is the start of his story moving away from that chapter in his life. This storyline brings Dick back to his roots to set his life on a new trajectory for this new series. Dick Grayson fans should enjoy this volume, but if you have no attachment to this character, I think you will find it an OK read.

Spoiler-filled review:

Issue One: This issue pretty much just sets the stage for the new status quo for Dick Grayson. He is Nightwing again, after a year of being Batman, but still living in Gotham. He visits his old circus friends from Haly’s, and gets attacked by a hired killer who says that Dick Grayson is one of the fiercest killers in all of Gotham and he doesn’t even know it.

Issue Two: Dick’s new, potential, love interest, Raya, was introduced in the last issue. In this issue, she bring him to see C.C. Haly, owner of Haly’s Circus, and he tells Dick that he knows that he is Nightwing, and that his destiny was supposed to be the circus. C.C. planned to one day leave the circus to Dick’s family, so now that he is dying, he leaves everything to Dick. The assassin from the last issue shows up and kills C.C. His name is Saiko, and he learned from C.C. the true identities of Batman and Nightwing. As C.C. lies dying he tells Dick that they are after him because of the secret about what the circus really is.

Issue Three: This was a good set up issue, but nothing so big happened that I felt like taking note of it along the way. It does sound like Dick plans to take over the circus though, so it will be obvious when the circus is traveling from city to city, and Nightwing shows up in every city where the circus is.

Issue Four: This issue features Batgirl, who I loved in the 1966 Batman TV show. DC Comics recently started a Superman/Wonder Woman series, but I’d rather see a Dick Grayson/Barbara Gordon series. They are great together!

Issue Five: The reveal here is that Saiko is actually Dick’s childhood friend, Raymond, and that he and Raya are playing Dick. Raymond has been “dead” for years, but appeared in Dick’s flashbacks throughout the last few issues. He didn’t seem that important during the flashbacks, so the reveal of who he was didn’t hit me that hard.

Issue Six: This issue sets the stage for the big finale. Plus, Alfred appears, but Bruce is still missing (not just from the book, but at this point in Batman’s storyline, he is actually missing).

Issue Seven: Nightwing defeats Saiko pretty easily, so not much to say about the final fight. The best part of this issue is when Bruce (who has returned) tells Dick that he was selected by the Court of Owls a long time ago to one day become a Talon (the name of their hired assassins). A a weird side note, Raymond and Raya now know that Batman is Bruce and Dick is Nightwing. Raymond looks to have died, but Raya is arrested. What is stopping her from spilling the beans on their secret identities?
top_profane.title: Dick Grayson fans should enjoy this volume, but if you have no attachment to this character, I think you will find it an OK read

top_profane.text:
I have enjoyed Dick Van Dyke's work since I was a child, especially "The Dick Van Dyke Show." I have the whole series on DVD and recently finished re-watching the entire series on Amazon Prime. Shortly afterward, I saw this Kindle book's price had dropped, so I took advantage of it. I found the book very interesting and read it quickly. Being a Midwesterner myself, I always remembered that Dick hailed from Danville, Illinois, but there was much I never knew about his personal life or his professional life prior to the series that launched his stardom. I loved reading about his experiences on the show and his thoughts about his co-stars. However, as some other reviewers have noted, some things were lacking regarding his personal life. For example, I would have liked to know more about his parents. His descriptions, especially of his mother, are quite sketchy. I also would have liked to know more about his first wife. He drops tidbits about her here and there, but I never felt like I got a full picture of her. He is more descriptive of his second -- well, not wife, because she would never get around to actually marrying him -- live-in, but I suppose it's natural that he would focus more on the woman he was with at the time.

I found this memoir insightful, but often the insights I gleaned were unintentional ones because Dick seems to lack the inclination toward introspection. For example, Dick says that he and Margie (his wife) never drank alcohol before coming to Hollywood. But gradually, they both started drinking socially and drank to the point that both became alcoholics requiring treatment. However, Dick never bothers to analyze why he began drinking in the first place -- caving to social pressure? -- and why the drinking became a crutch for him. Was it the pressures of his career? Difficulties at home? Feelings of insecurity? We don't know because Dick either isn't curious himself or doesn't wish to reveal it. He talks about his smoking addiction and describes several fruitless attempts to quit. He basically says that he *couldn't* quit. However, when an X-ray revealed a potential health problem, he quit immediately and never smoked again. What this revealed is that he had the capacity to quit all along, but he didn't have sufficient motivation to quit until the X-ray. Once he wanted to quit more than he wanted to smoke, he succeeded. But I don't think he quite grasped that himself.

Dick says he is really a lot like Rob Petrie -- or rather, the reverse. The writers used Dick's personality to develop the character of Rob. Dick said that, like Rob, he is very non-confrontational and is a people pleaser. He said that Rose Marie dubbed him the "six-foot tower of Jello." I find it interesting, therefore, that he describes the two women in his life, Margie and Michelle, as strong women. It has been my observation that non-aggressive and compliant men often gravitate to strong (sometimes domineering) women. Ironically, it would appear that Margie's strong personality worked against her in the end. She did not like the Hollywood culture, making no bones about it, and therefore did not take as active an interest in Dick's career as he would have liked. He became involved with another strong women who relished Hollywood and everything about it. So Dick was drawn away to someone who took a more active interest in his career and wanted to participate in that aspect of his life. It would seem that Dick's amenable personality led him to gradually be drawn into an adulterous relationship until he reached the point where he felt there was no turning back and wanted to divorce Margie. Though Dick's memoir never really allowed me to make an emotional connection to Margie, I couldn't help but feel sorry for this woman who had been married to Dick since he was a nobody and bore him several children, only to be discarded when another woman came onto the scene who, perhaps, took advantage of a famous man going through a mid-life crisis. Although Dick thought Michelle was wonderful, I just don't come away with the same impression. YMMV.

Throughout the book, Dick stresses his desire to live up to his mother's admonition to be a good and decent person. I think in most ways he did achieve that. But he never really acknowledged how his alcoholism and marital infidelity played into that. Also, Dick talks about being a church goer for much of his life, and not only that but also a Sunday school teacher and an elder in the Presbyterian church. However, it's clear that Dick never had a true understanding of what Christianity actually is. The gospel seems to have eluded him. Instead of realizing that the Christian faith is centered on repenting of sin and accepting the sacrifice Christ made on the cross, Dick still focuses on being a good person as his means of salvation -- that is, being good enough for God rather than realizing nobody is good enough, which is why we need God's forgiveness and grace. This explains why he fell away from the church. When an issue came up within his church that revealed a very un-Christian attitude by those in authority, he decided the church was the problem instead of those particular people. It didn't seem to occur to him to look for another church that had a more Christ-like attitude toward all people. Instead, he became disillusioned and dropped out. Very sad.

Dick Van Dyke is an immensely talented actor who continues to give me and countless others great enjoyment through his work. At his core, he is a man with solid Midwestern values who, in some ways, was led astray by the Hollywood culture. He displays a lack of self-awareness in some areas of his life, but he comes across as genuine and likeable, just as he does on screen. His memoir is not the best of its genre, but it is generally a fun and interesting read, and I'm sure most fans will find it worthwhile.
top_profane.title: Insightful memoir, yet lacking in some ways

top_profane.text:
Declan - Book #1

Brooke - works in the office of the strip club of her uncle (who took care of she and her sister when they were young.

Angel - Brooke's sister

Ashley - friend and neighbor of Brooke

Devon - Brook and Angel's uncle who helped raise them

Declan - VP of Soulless Bastards MC

Blu - Declan's uncle who took over the Soulless Bastards MC when Declan's father was killed

--------------------------

For some strange reason, at least in Brooke's estimation, Ashley likes sleep overs with her friends, even though they are all in their early 20'gives up and joins them. Brooke isn't thrilled tonight because she doesn't really care for the rest of the girls Ashley invited BUT she knows if she doesn't go, Ashley and all the others will camp out on her porch until she gives up and joins them.

During a game of Truth or Dare Brooke takes a Dare . . . and she gets dared to go to the Bikers Clubhouse down the street and get a beer from one of them. When asked if she needs to get her jeans on she's told she can wear what she has on . . . booty shorts that she wears at pajamas. So she grabs her chucks, zips up her hoodie and takes off down the street.

She's stopped by prospect and talks him into giving her the beer he is drinking while on lookout duty. She meets Declan when he comes out to find out why the prospect is talking to a chick when he is supposed to be on lookout duty. While chewing out the prospect Declan is checking her out in her booty shorts.

He does a LOT more than check out her shorts that night.
------------
Mayhem - Book #2

Mayhem - nicknamed by his mom because it fit him, member of Soulless Bastards MC, son of the Blu

Blu - president of the Soulless Bastards MC

Taylor Masters- daughter of the local mayor, Bill, who has been in the pockets of the MC for years. He wants them to guard her while she is home.

Beth - Taylor's only friend at home

April - owns the diner, Beth suggests Taylor get a job there until she knows what she wants

Freddy - Beth's brother who is the cook at April's diner

Tic - Blu's son half brother to Mayhem, member of Soulless Bastards MC

-------------
Taylor comes home from Rome. Her father had sent her to school in Europe, having blamed her for her mother's death from cancer (when she was 7) and about everything else that had happened in their home. She's not happy. All she feels in the house are all the memories she has. She would rather have her own place to live.

She ends up in her own apartment, along with her Chihuahua, Minnie, that she brought back with her from Rome. Once her dad leaves there's a knock at her door, when she goes to answer it there is a big guy staring at her with his mouth open. She asks who he is and he asks if she is Taylor Masters.

He realizes she has blonde hair and blue eyes, which reminds him of Becca, the love of his life who died 4 years ago and he can't come out of his grieving for her.

She keeps on his case about about his feet on her coffee table, and anything else she can think of. She isn't impressed with his dress . . . says he looks like he should be on a corner selling drugs, which he responds with "No, but I can get you anything you want." He's really pushing her buttons with his attitude. When she wants to know how long he's staying, we find out he is on night shift and finds out the couch is his for the night.

Taylor's luck just gets better . . she's carrying a tray of food, turns around and smacks right into Tic, sending food and dishes on him and the floor . . . not only is she embarrassed she now has "the hots" for not only Freddy but Tic.

That night, when she gets home, Mayhem is there. One of his brothers calls to see if he is coming to the party, Monster just patched in so they are celebrating. He says no, but Taylor overhears him and asks to go . . . she is getting a little stir crazy just hanging out in the apartment He tells her to get something different on because he knows his brothers will be all over her in her small waitress uniform. She gets changed, Mayhem still thinks she may get hit on so he tells her to stay close.

Later he finds her with Brooke and Cherry and knows she is safe with them. The surprise is on him . . . he finds out Brooke offered her a job at LEGGS and she takes it . . . as a stripper. Mayhem is NOT HAPPY AT ALL ! ! ! !

-------------------

Tic - Book #3
Tic - son of Blu, president of the Soulless Bastards MC, half brother to Mayhem

Jameson - Tic's 5 year old son whose other left when he was 3 months old

Ashley - Brooke's best friend

Kenderly - raised in the Soulless Bastards MC, has something going on with Blu but no one is sure what
----------------

Ashley just got fired from her job. No, wait, she quit before she was fired She ends up going to the Soulless Bastards MC to meet up with Brooke. Here she becomes the "shield" to hide Jameson while he runs from his Uncle Declan . . . of course Jameson giggles the whole time.

When Ashley hears that Jameson needs a babysitter, Tic fired the last one for continuing to show him scary movies (Blu said he should "keep it in his pants" when it comes to babysitters anyway. Ashley volunteers When she forgets she doesn't know if Jameson has allergies or his doctors numbers she runs out to catch Tic before he roars off When he tells her she returns to the clubhouse where Jameson is waiting on her and takes her hand Tic is dumbfounded. Jameson has NEVER done this before.

Ashley has worked for Ti about a month when he comes home and realizes she is still cleaning the house when she doesn't have to and cooking supper for not only Jameson but has his ready when he gets home. Tonight he asks her to sit and eat with him which she does. While they are eating they hear something from down the hall They both take off and find Jameson getting sick.

Ashley takes Jameson in the bathroom and starts cleaning him up. As she waits for Tic to grab him some clean pj's, Tic gets a call and has to head out Ashley says she an stay until he gets back. Whennit is taking longer than she realized, she takes a shower and rummages in Tics dresser for a t-shirt and shorts (her clothes had aught a lot of Jameson's puke).

When Tic gets home he realizes he wants her to stay, he asks and she says okay . . . now the story really begins.

-----------------
Prospect - Book #4

Nathanial Dillinger (AKA Nuts)- a Prospect with Soulless Bastards MC, he is being groomed by Declan

Declan - the new President of the Soulless Bastards MC after Blu was killed, he was basically raised by Blu

Cherry Murphy- she supposedly is a "typical club whore" (her mother had been the same - no one knew her her father was - she was born and raised in the clubhouse) but she has a heart of gold and will help anyone who needs help . . . as long as she can give you some sass

Prim - has grown up in the Soulless Bastards MC Clubhouse
--------

Unlike most of the members and Prospects of the Soulless Bastards, MC, Nathanial grew up in a wealthy family, the typical "Boy Next Door" who was given everything on a "silver platter." he was spoiled and hated it. He rebelled against his parents, wanting to earn his way in life, and at 18 he showed up at the Soulless Bastards Clubhouse.

- - - - - -

There is so much more that happens in this story but I got so hooked on the reading I forgot to update my review. But, trust me, this is THE BEST of the Soulless Bastards MC Series. It kind of pulls a lot together from the first three books.
top_profane.title: The People are Hard Core BUT They protect their Families to the fullest.

top_profane.text:
*Minor Spoilers*

Black Cloud Rising follows Sergeant Richard Etheridge of the African Brigade on their mission liberating Plantations in coastal North Carolina, as well as flushing out remaining Confederates and partisan guerillas. The story takes place in 1863, in a time and place where many enslaved men had fled their masters to join up with the Union Army on its peninsula campaign. Richard “Dick” Etheridge is one such man, along with a few childhood friends. Other refugees escape to a free encampment under the protection of the Union Army, including Dick’s sweetheart Fanny.

While Black Cloud Rising focuses on a specific historical backdrop and series of events, I felt the narrative to be more about Dick’s internal struggle with identity (although of course his identify is also shaped by the context of the time.) Sgt. Etheridge anchors the story, while other characters seem to come and go with few lingering moments. I felt I didn’t really get to know many of the other characters in detail.

Dick is the son of an enslaved women and her master; that is, his master is his biological father. Such entanglements were unfortunately common in that a master could literally "grow his wealth” and “property” by creating new humans to enslave (regardless of blood ties) with unwilling women who had no choice in the matter. Understandably, Dick struggles with this aspect of his heritage. While he knows his mother is right in her views of keeping self worth and respect, and the way in which she raises him to be his own man, at times there is still a part of him that seeks to please and have the approval of his “white family.” I use “family” loosely here, since blood does not always mean everything and/or anything. In the way a narcissist gaslights his victims, the Etheridges by their actions and words do the same with Dick by teaching him to read and write, praising his cleverness, and telling him that he is essentially “one of the good ones.” Sarah Etheridge teaches him to read, while Patrick Etheridge acts as a childhood playmate and brother. At the same time, Dick is also keenly aware that to them, he will only ever just be at the top of a group they view to be sub-human–i.e., he will never be viewed by them with true equality and respect.

While Dick struggles with these issues of identity and internalized racism, he participates in the liberating of nearby plantations. Enslaved folk join the African Brigade column as they march through the swampy areas of coastal North Carolina. Witnessing the hardship these people have gone through, Dick's self-doubt returns in comparing their experiences to his own. Dick often comes up against fellow soldier Revere, who is unapologetically himself and calls out Dick on his tendency to please and seek the approval of their white officers (the brigade was led by General Wild.) Under the command of General Wild, the newly liberated are given the opportunity to exact justice upon their former masters–-one such incident that was particularly stand out in its turning of tables was when an elderly woman, bent and broken from a lifetime of slavery, is given a whip to lash her former master.

While on campaign, Dick finds out that Patrick Etheridge has joined the rebel guerillas and briefly reunites with him, however, I found the meeting to be somewhat anticlimactic. Patrick is let go and seemingly never faces the consequences to his previous actions. This situation mirrors that of even Union soldiers and Union supporting civilians who treat Dick and the rest of the African Brigade with disdain.

Overall I felt Black Cloud Rising excelled most in depicting Dick’s internal struggles with his identity, but the plot, blink and miss it B plots, and side characters seemed not as fleshed out and detailed as they could have been. What’s more, I felt the story moved too fast (not necessarily in a good way) and rushed past/glossed over events that could have been dug into deeper. However, I appreciated Dick’s story (as it was and is no doubt a common theme in the study of racism in America,) as well as learning about the African Brigade’s feats in North Carolina in 1863.
top_profane.title: Character Study

top_profane.text:
“Now as a man is like this or like that, according as he acts and according as he behaves, so will he be; a man of good acts will become good, a man of bad acts, bad; he becomes pure by pure deeds, bad by bad deeds; and here they say that a person consists of desires, and as is his desire, so is his will; and as is his will, so is his deed; and whatever deed he does, that he will reap.” ~ Brihadaranyaka Upanishad, 7th Century BC

Karma is a bit difficult to define, especially for those of us who think in terms of western philosophy, but the essence of it is that the Universe will balance itself out, one way or the other. Our actions, good or bad, affect not only us, but everyone around us and they will come back to us. Whether they enhance our lives or tear them apart, is directly determined by our contribution to the Universe. J.J. is a good man, not perfect, but a person with good intentions, a man who treats everyone as fairly as possible. When Dick crosses his path, J.J., in good faith, accepts his offer of friendship. Never could he have imagined the wickedness and havoc that Dick wreaks, not just in J.J.s life, but in everyone's with whom he comes into contact.

J.J. has a disadvantage when it comes to understanding someone's true intentions. He's not had the nurturing that most of us have as a child, therefore, he's never learned how to love or trust with his whole heart. He's never experienced the deep, secure feeling of having someone have your back, at least not as an ordinary person has. His inter-personal relations, starting with his family, have been abusive, obscured, or warped in one way or another. When it comes to his association with Dick, he lets him set the parameters because he has no real sense of self. J.J. doesn't know what he can handle or not, and, maybe at one level or another, what he actually deserves. This mindset provides fertile ground for Dick's manipulative behavior, making it even easier to bend J.J. to his will. Dick keeps him confused; he dances circles around J.J.’s gullibility. Dick keeps him off balance, draws him in, and before J.J. knows it, he's in too deep to see his way out. By then, he's embarrassed and angry at himself, besides being hurt and feeling used. One thing Dick counts on about J.J. is his moral compass. Regardless of what Dick does, he's instilled in J.J. an, albeit misplaced, sense of loyalty, trusting that will keep J.J. hooked. There's no way to win with a person like that. Fortunately, the Universe's intentions begin to shift and put Alex into J.J.’s path. Alex becomes his nurturer, in body and in spirit, and helps J.J. see beyond the negativity and helps him find his inner peace; J.J. learns to shine his light brighter than ever before.

At many points in the story, I wanted to tie a rock around Dick's ankle and throw him in the ocean, just to be rid of him. It's not a very enlightened attitude, but Dick is...well, a dick! At first I felt only anger and righteous indignation for the way he used J.J. Watching Dick insinuate himself into J.J.’s life was nothing less than excruciating. I had a difficult time getting through the first several chapters because it was so disturbing, but then I started to comprehend that, in actuality, Dick even believes his lies. He can't admit, even to himself that he is gay. He, in essence, is deluding himself as much, if not more, than anyone else in his life. As the story continues, Dick is slowly, but surely, destroying his own life, without any help at all. All the facades he puts up, all the games and deceptions are just as damaging to himself. When he gets to the point where he has to face his consequences, only then does he learn just how poorly he played his own game, fooling no one.

This is a strong book with several important life lessons which Dan wove expertly into this real-life inspired tale. We've all had Dicks in our lives, people we aren't sure how to deal with, who infuriate and disgust us; who threaten to turn us inside out, but what's most important about a situation like that, is how we deal with them. As high-minded as it seems, people can only affect you as much as you let them. I recommend this book to anyone who enjoys a solid, thought-provoking, and often too close for comfort look at life. Thank you , Dan, for sharing your talent and your honest presentation of your own life experiences.

Originally reviewed by Rainbow Book Reviews.
top_profane.title: The Price of Dick by Dan Skinner

top_profane.text:
My method for success

This was my second attempt at reading Moby Dick and the success of this second attempt is attributable to two things:

1) Reading it on a Kindle

2) Using the Moby Dick Big Read Along website [...]

In regards to reading the ebook version of this novel, using both a dedicated Kindle and the Kindle app on mu iPhone made it incredibly easy simply for the integrated dictionary. The first failed reading of this novel happened when I picked up a lovely Easton Press leather hardback. I sat and cracked open the book and by the third chapter I realized I would be needing a dictionary handy. I never got farther than that in my first attempt and this second attempt was only made successful for not having to stop to look up the hundreds of archaic words. There were still quite a few that my integrated dictionary couldn't fin, but taking the time to look these up was far less damaging to the reading experience than had I gone with the print version. A word of caution: the free version of the ebook I read (ASIN:B004TRXX7C) had errors whenever it dealt with lists of numbers i.e. list of money valuations, weights, etc. Frequently the text was egregiously wrong with the first number in the list.

Using the Moby Dick Big Read Along website was also crucial to my finishing this novel. This website was suggested by author Joe Hill as he commenced his reading of this novel. The site is dedicated to a free podcast reading of the novel with each chapter being read by someone different. There were only a few chapters I could not listen to (because of their style of reading) but on the whole, reading the ebook along with this verbal reading made the experience magnitudes better. There are times in the novel when it was not clear to me that a dialog was taking place, but the vocal performances made it explicitly clear. Also, Melville abuses commas and semicolons like a champ and the vocal readings made it much easier to find the flow of sentence than deciphering it out on my own. Added bonus is that there are a few celebrity readers such as Tilda Swinton and Benedict Cumberbatch.

My experience

This is a hard read but a rewarding one. Yet, I would caution the reader that if you're looking for a thriller - fast paced and exciting - this is not the book for it. There is action but it is far outweighed by the academics of whaling. On the whole, my experience with this book is to, at a very high level, say it is the grandfather of tales such as the movie Jaws. There's a fellow that knows all the biology of the animal (Hooper/Ishmael), a fellow that wants to keep everyone safe and kill the beast but not at the expense of his own life (Brody/Starbuck) and the fellow that will stop at nothing to see this beast dead by his own hands (Quint/Ahab).

However, most of the book is dedicated not to the backbone of the Jaws storyline but rather to the documentation of whaling. Much like the book 20,000 Leagues Under The Seas, there are entire chapters (several actually) that take painstaking care in documenting the biology and happenings of the time - the anatomy of a whale, the various species of whale, the history of whale depictions, the process of hunting for whale, the process of dismantling a whale, the worth of whale parts (oil, ambergris, bone, etc) and exacting detail of the components of a whaling ship. It feels more like a biology lesson rather than a novel and in this day in age, it really made me wonder why anyone would go through so much trouble to hunt a whale. However as tedious as these parts can be, I found it incredibly interesting... almost as if it were a tome to a dying art. These sailors were the Deadliest Catch fishermen of their day and age. They suffered weather, rations, tediousness, physical hardship, sharks, and seclusion in hopes of bringing home enough oil to sell and receive their share. Many did not come home (or like Ahab didn't come home whole) and that was just part of expectations of such a hazardous profession.

But if you can slog through those parts, the pay off is incredible. All that I knew about this novel (and that most people will hear about this novel) only takes place in the last three chapters of this 135 chapter novel. But the characters are so rich and interesting. The harpooners, Queequeg, Tashtego and Daggoo are my favorite characters because they are so different from anyone else. Ishmael, our narrator, is an interesting fellow himself. He seems to have a bit of jading toward religion ("I'll try a pagan friend, thought I, since Christian kindness has proved but hollow courtesy." p. 46) but in all other things seems to be a fair narrator by conveying more of what he sees rather than commentary about what he sees. And of course there is steady Starbuck and our mad Captain Ahab. Ahab is more of a brooding figure that does not prominently play into the action until well into the second half of the novel. Ahab has a singular determination for revenge. Above the profit of the ship, the lives of his crew and even above his own life itself, Ahab's singular purpose for this voyage is to find and kill Moby Dick. He is so tortured by his first encounter with the White Whale, that revenge consumes him to the point that he no longer wishes to live if it means he must endure Moby Dick living as well. It is an all consuming hatred that drives Ahab and that in the end costs him everything. It is proof positive that frequently, hatred consumes the person rather than the object of the hate.

Similar to Ahab, Moby Dick himself doesn't make an appearance in the novel until the antepenultimate chapter. Much like the movie Jaws, you'll have to wait quite some time to actually see the monster in all his glory. In Ahab's obsession over Moby Dick, I found it intriguing that Ahab truly believed that Moby Dick can hold a grudge and have a single mind for attacking rather than fleeing a capture. Whales apparently normally don't attack with viciousness, yet this is exactly what happens upon meeting the unique creature.

The short of it - if you want to take a shortcut, read just the last three chapters and that will get you about 90% of everything you've ever heard about this book. If you want the full experience, I would strongly suggest taking your time reading it and augmenting a print version with an audio version. It helps immensely.

Popular CultureThe thing I love most about reading classics is it becomes clear that all works of art are somehow connected or based on others. Slogging through and enduring the bulk of the archaic and There were several instances of this in Moby Dick.

On two occasions, the movie Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan echoed lines from Moby Dick. The first was when addressing his first mate Starbuck and the listening crew, Ahab confirms that it was indeed Moby Dick that took his leg. Displaying all his enmity for the whale, Ahab shows to what lengths he is willing to go to hunt down and kill him when he declares,

"I'll chase him round Good Hope, and round the Horn, and round the Norway Maelstrom, and round perdition's flames before I give him up."

- CHAPTER 36 The Quarter-Deck (p. 150)

The second came at the end of the book. Ahab again utters a familiar quote. Similar to Khan, in his last moments of life, Ahab says,

"Towards thee I roll, thou all-destroying but unconquering whale; to the last I grapple with thee; from hell's heart I stab at thee; for hate's sake I spit my last breath at thee."

- CHAPTER 135 The Chase - Third Day (p.538)

The last pop culture reference I found in Moby Dick was a short but lovely song that the harpooners and sailors were singing on the Pequod - and it's because I first heard it from Quint in the movie Jaws:

"Farewell and adieu to you, Spanish ladies!

Farewell and adieu to you, ladies of Spain!"

- CHAPTER 40 Midnight, Forecastle (p. 158)

Finding these gems not only made Moby Dick more fun, but it also gave me an appreciation for the other works and characters who referenced the lines from the novel and in the case of Star Trek II: The Wrath of Khan, the depth of what they were saying by using these references.

In all, I wanted the feeling of accomplishment for reading a classic that is so frequently referenced and I can say definitively that I have that. It was an amazing month long journey through a book that I fear will pass into unreadability because of it's archaic nature and we would be all the poorer for it.
top_profane.title: Accomplished

top_profane.text:
This is part 4 of the crossover that is happening between all the batbooks, night of the monster men. I already read part 5 of this crossover as well, which is nightwing #6. Don't really know why the Batman issues that are apart of this crossover are getting so much praise when the Batman issues probably have to be the weakest out of this whole event. My problem with this crossover is mostly with how underused nightwing was in all of this, he definitely got the short end of the stick just like he usually, always has, night of the monster men had so much potential to be just a really fun crossover and I get that they aren't going to be acting like all is well considering that they believe that Tim is dead, I get that, so maybe dc should have held off on this crossover until Tim had returned, Steve Orlando is trying his best with what he has to make this crossover fun, however I'm just scratching my head as to why Steve Orlando would make the only character besides steph that could had made this crossover a lot more fun, why he would just sideline him and turn him into a monster, since steph wasn't going to provide the positive attitude, a lot more nightwing would have been nice, now that I've mentioned steph I'm really confused as to why Steve Orlando is writing her as if she's the only one who lost Tim, as much as I like steph someone really needs to tell her that she isn't the only one, also a lot the things Steve Orlando said regarding Dick grayson and what he said he was going to do with him in this crossover didn't happen, which is why I was so excited about this crossover in the first place, now I'm just really disappointed and am going to ignore anything he says about the character no matter how good it sounds. Also that comment that batwoman made towards Dick and gotham girl, really Dick, at the beginning of this issue "I've whipped the rest of the family into shape where were you ...its time for a lesson" rubbed me the wrong like yes batwoman is a badass and I can see her pretty much talk down on any Batman character except Dick Grayson, I just can't see her pulling rank on him.

There have been some positives in this crossover though like this crossover really made me like gotham girl, I really like the pacing of the story, the art in the Batman issues were great, and I really like the way Steve Orlando has been writing Dick grayson, he really seems to understand just who Dick is, I enjoy it so much that I would much rather have Steve Orlando take over nightwing then have Tim seeley continue to write it, also really like that he understands that Dick is the only person who knows Bruce the best, pre new 52 I would have said Alfred, then Dick, and then Clark however in the new 52 and so far in rebirth it's definitely Dick, nightwing also very much felt like the outcast in this crossover but after part 5 he started to really fit in, the steph and Dick team up could have been so much better, I'm so mad that Steve Orlando did not take advantage of the fact that this is a crossover and could have had more characters that don't interact team up, we could have had some really emotional moments between steph and dick, I just wanted to scream in my pillow because if the writers would let them Dick and steph could become great friends, they are so much alike, even Bruce has mentioned before how much steph reminds him of Dick. Overall this event has been pretty disappointing so really unless something big happens in the next issue, which is the final part to this crossover and comes out next Wednesday in detective comics #942, this crossover is going to be pretty forgettable. Rating 4/10

I'll start this review with a reference given at the end of the book. In the Acknowledgements section to be exact. Author Carson Vaughan was with his future wife traveling along the small town backroads of her youth when she casually pointed out a spot off the highway and said: "that's where Reuben was shot." Having written two books myself, I know that inspiration can come from unexpected sources. Learning that Reuben was a chimpanzee sparked Carson's interest. What started out as a college thesis project morphed into a narrative nonfiction manuscript some ten-plus years in the making.

Carson Vaughan was right about his initial hunch. The story of Reuben and the events leading to his death is a compelling slice-of-life tale. The manic events surrounding Reuben's death are given in great detail in the final chapters of the book. Everything that precedes that event makes up the lion's share of the story. How is it that a chimpanzee ended up as the star attraction of a second-rate zoo in Nebraska? Who are the people behind these road-side zoos? What is like trying to run a zoo with limited funds? These are some of the questions covered in Zoo Nebraska.

The genesis of Zoo Nebraska starts with Dick Haskin, who brought Reuben to Nebraska in the bed of his pickup truck. Dick was set on a course of primatology research. His plans were cut short when Dian Fossey, who Dick was slated to assist in Rwanda, was murdered. Dick had no intentions of starting a Zoo. In fact, he despised zoos. But Dick wasn't the best at turning his interests into income. When he saw an opportunity to charge admission for others to view Reuben, Zoo Nebraska was born.

Despite Dick Haskin's good intentions, Reuben lived a life of isolation and confinement. Dick had plans for a large enclosure where Reuben and companion chimps could live a more natural life, but Dick didn't have the funds to build such an enclosure. His attempts at finding other chimpanzees to join Reuben came up short. Dick barely had funds to feed himself. But Dick had a dream and he made strides towards fulfilling that dream one day at a time.

As more animals were added to the Zoo, Dick's responsibilities grew. Eventually, the demands on Dick exceeded his abilities and desires and he burned himself out. This is the point where Dick leaves the Zoo and others enter the picture. The new caretakers quickly arrange for several companion chimps to join Reuben, who by this time was showing signs of severe depression. Despite some early successes and promising developments, such as a $50,000 donation from Johnny Carson, the new Zoo caretakers are no more successful than Dick.

The fight to keep the Zoo going comes to a head when during a routine cleaning of the chimp's cages the chimps are inadvertently set free. Any thoughts that free-roaming chimpanzees is not a big deal are put to rest when the author describes another incident where two chimps got free and attacked a man and his wife, disfiguring the man.

Reuban's final day is both sad and hopeful. What were his intentions? Was he filled with rage and ready to attack anyone who got in his way? Or was he feeling glad that he was finally free? Free of his cage? Free of perpetual confinement? Free to spend his last moments on earth to go and do whatever he pleased. At one point during the escape, one of the chimps stops by a nearby house to swing on a tire hung from a tree. That is the image that I'd like to think describes their mindset at the time.

I read the book on a Kindle and listened on Audible. I was disappointed to discover that there wasn't a single image to accompany the story. Perhaps there were images in the printed version. Whatever the excuse, the lack of images was disheartening. No matter how much description the author provided, I never formed a picture in my head of the zoo except for a collection of cages. To not even have a picture of Reuben is ridiculous.
top_profane.title: The life and death of Reuben the chimpanzee
