top_profane.title: Crap

top_profane.text:
Spoilers ahead!

To sum up this book in Gabe's favorite word?

Damn.

Honestly, I expected to really enjoy this story. Maya Banks writes really well, but lately her books have been a total miss. This one? It is so far beyond a miss it didn't even hit the edges of the target and I still can't even find the arrow. I just hope it is in Gabe Hamilton's rear.

First of all, it seems that all of Maya's heroes sound the same once you've read one. Read one, and you've read them all, and this one, despite being a billionaire, keeps sounding like a "damn" trucker ready to be cast in Honey Booboo. I love alpha heroes - Rush from Fallen Too Far, Gideon Cross - but this one was not only alpha, he was a TWAD.

After waiting so many years to move in on his friend's sister, Gabe decides to do it behind his friend's back and make the kill when the friend is out of the city. Wow. Not cool, buddy. Why don't you man up at 38 and just tell your friend you want his sister? Oh, I know, because deep in your soul, you're a girl. You can take on Mia because you've made her signed off her life to you and her body, but clearly you don't have the b*lls to take on your friend and man up to what you want.

So. The contract. He asks her to enter a damn contract that basically gives him ownership of her body, and this damn contract is ultimately a motive not to protect her, but to protect himself for getting too extreme in the damn bedroom and having been previously charged with sexual charges of damn abuse from a crazy ex. Which do not surprise me once I get to know this gem of a man. Honestly, Gabe is so gross, I think the ex might have been onto something. He treats Mia coldly most of the damn time and she just allows it, to the point where she is humiliated and humiliated and used and used again. Damn.

I enjoy a good submissive book (like Captive in the Dark, which is cruel and raw but completely engrossing), but this is not a good submissive book at all. The man is the worst "master" figure I could imagine, breaking damn promises to her and assuming zero responsibility for his damn dominant role in this kind of relationship. He is the most out of control damn hero I've ever had the mispleasure of reading! There wasn't even a single sex scene where he was truly tender. In fact there was hardly a scene where he allowed her to reach the peak before HE did. Selfish much? That's because you are, Mr. Hamilton. The most selfish twad I've had the damn pleasure of reading. It was all about what you wanted, what you damn well liked and wanted your little sex slave to do to. You put the self in selfish, Gabe, and you will be added to Webster's dictionary under the word TWAD for good.

So our damn billionaire pedophile Gabe says that he is a dominant but that he doesn't need a safe word because "no" will do. (My thoughts about this? : What a missed opportunity here for us to see some real intimacy between them. Even the responsibility of that shared word, he didn't want. To dominants and submissives, "no" is a game. No means yes. The safe word is the real no. Why would anyone want to be in that sort of relationship without enjoying one of the most all-consuming fantasy of all ages, which is "saying no but meaning yes." The safe word gives you that, but Mr. Hamilton says "no" is good enough for him. And yet once again it is an effort to keep this relationship from being true BDSM.) Can I say damn one more time? He sure as damn does. So there.

Back to the issue . . .
Our DAMN TWAD is clearly no true dominant, for most of the time with her he is out of control and even frets about it later. Whoever likes to see a dominant fretting about what he did to his submissive? Not a single damn soul! Dominants are supposed to know their exact roles with their submissives and they should stay within those lines! But our Damn Twad is no true master. He is the master of twad and damn only. So this book is no BDSM, but it is also not a regular sexy romance, so it ends up being an awful mix that - despite some hot sex scenes - left me feeling empty. Especially after the scene in Paris which to me was painful to read. Here, he wants to prove to his damn self he doesn't care, and just completely makes a damn decision that makes me want to smack him and Mia for signing her damn life away to him, as well.

Mia forgives him too soon to my damn liking (which was never) and pretty soon he's whipping her only to get caught by . . . oops. And damn. It's her brother. How utterly HUMILIATING. Since when is wearing damn plugs during WORK and being taken in the damn mouth until she weeps real damn tears and being shared beyond what you imagined is damn sexy? This is so far beyond sexy, I felt greasy and dirty for days, and all because Mr. Twad is such a bad hero. I like anti-heroes but he is not even a good anti-hero. He is NO hero. Period. And damn.
top_profane.title: Disappointed in it

top_profane.text:
Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap.Holy crap. Holy crap. Holy crap.

It's like JLA knew that I wasn't super in love with TCOGB, so she gave me this beautiful, wonderful gift of a book. I loved it! While I'm still not a huge fan of her method of sharing worldbuilding history (lots of confusing conversations), her plot, action, and romance make me very happy.

And the good thing is, the way this book ended, I'm not dying on some major cliffhanger.

I think that's all I'll share before I get a full review up. But one last thing: Kieran, I love you.

4.5/5 stars
top_profane.title: Holy crap.

top_profane.text:
When I read that Dan Skinner had a new novel out, I knew it was a “must” read. I had read Memorizing You, and it was outstanding. Then I read the description of The Price of Dick and I realized this was going to be a very different book than the sweet coming of age story in Memorizing You. It is, and the author brings his gift of storytelling to a more adult themed book with glimpses into his world as a photographer and writer.

J.J. Johnstone is a photographer, aspiring writer and is fairly happy with his life at the moment. He has recently ended a long term relationship on amicable terms, and is not looking for a serious relationship. When he spots Richard Fitch at the gym, it is instantattraction (we learn that Richard would rather be called Dick – and this should have been a red flag, but I am getting ahead of myself). Richard is one of those guys that, physically, really appeals to J.J. and eventually they get together. It was not what J.J. expected and decides if they never hookup again, that is fine, but Dick shows up at his door with a pitiful story and ends up working his way into J.J.’s life.

Dick has issues. He is gay but in denial, he enjoys playing games with people’s emotions and has no scruples. J.J. can see these things, although he tends to minimize them. He is stil looking for the best in Dick even when people are telling him to get away from this man. As the relationship continues, more and more of J.J.’s life is lost to the façade that Dick builds around himself and J.J. finds himself being pushed into a closet he said he would never live in again, not since he left home as a teenager.

Dan Skinner is a wonderful storyteller. In The Price of Dick, the story has so much truth to it, of how we can allow toxic people into our lives. How easy it is to live in denial about that person, when we want so badly for something to be right that we are willing to ignore the warning signs and get caught in the downward spiral where we lose ourselves in the another person’s baggage. It is easy to see why J.J. is drawn to Dick. Dick brings things into J.J.’s life that seem to enrich it - but only in the short term and really it is only for Dick’s benefit, not to help J.J.. Dick is selfish and manipulative and plays on people’s weaknesses. J.J. is a good man, looking for the good in people, trying to build his business and be happy. He has no family and his friend network is limited. When things start to get bad, there is no one to turn to. At first. But you have to read the book to find out more.

Dan Skinner’s writing vividly brings his characters to life and had me emotionally invested in their outcome. There are many wonderful characters and moments in the book. From the very beginning I was drawn into the story. I had moments of disbelief – how could anyone behave like that (Dick) and ‘Oh why do you let him do these things?’ (J.J.). It is an interesting, and sometimes painful, journey we take with J.J., but we also get to experience a wonderful transformation with him as well. I loved how he takes back his life and it was a lovely experience for me as a reader.

Originally posted at "Live Your Life, Buy The Book"
top_profane.title: another winner from Skinner!!

top_profane.text:
** WARNING: This review contains 'Lover Reborn' spoilers, and spoilers of all other BDB books! **

** In-Between **

`Lover Reborn' is the tenth book in J.R. Ward's paranormal `Black Dagger Brotherhood' series . . . `Reborn' is one of the most-anticipated books of this long-running series because it is Tohrment's story, and it has been a long time coming.

One year ago Tohrment's life was ripped apart. His pregnant shellan, Wellsandra, was murdered by the lesser society and Tohr has been mourning her and their young ever since. Immediately after her death he took himself away from the Brotherhood, but was dragged back by the angel Lassiter whose job it was to see Tohr through his dark time.

For many books now, Tohr has been a shell of his former self. He has watched his brothers find their own shellan's, as he grows embittered by empty memories of his two-hundred-year mating with Wellsie. Recently, Tohr's adopted son, John Matthew, found his own shellan in the form of half-sympath vampire, Xhex. But Xhex hasn't been the only new female addition to the Brotherhood mansion . . . following the events of `Lover Mine' which revealed Xhex's origins, her long-lost mother also came to stay with the Brotherhood.

Rosalhynda was a female of worth, once upon a time. Until a sympath kidnapped and raped her, and impregnated her during her needing. Hundreds of years ago Tohrment and his mentor, Darius, rescued Rosalhynda and spent months keeping her safe during her pregnancy. . . but after giving birth to Xhex, Rosalhynda took her own life with a dagger to the gut - so full of shame for her fall from grace, she could not go on living, not even for her young.

Unbeknownst to Tohr and Xhex, Rosalhynda did not go into the Fade. Instead she went to serve The Chosen and Scribe Virgin, going over to the other side and renaming herself No'One - donning a hooded robe and dedicating herself to a life of servitude as penance for her sins.

Now No'One is back, and living in the Brotherhood Mansion with orders to serve the Chosen female, Payne, but also to reconnect with her estranged daughter, Xhex.

But No'One's presence is rubbing salt in Tohr's wound. No'One has seemingly returned from the dead, permitted to have another life on earth . . . yet his Wellsie and their young are gone forever, lost to him in the Fade. Or so he thought.

Lassiter finally decides to break his silence and explain to Tohr that Wellsie and their son have not migrated to the Fade - they are not in peace. Instead, they are in-between. Neither here nor there, in a barren grey landscape, tethered only because Tohrment cannot let them go.

If Tohr wants his family to find peace in the Fade, he must find peace here on earth. And he might just find it in the most unlikely of places, with No'One.

** Autumn **

J.R. Ward had such a big task on her hands, writing Tohr's story. She had a lot of balls to juggle in getting his HEA just right, and that's partly why his story has taken so long to write. The Warden had to ensure that enough time had passed since Wellsie's death, before Tohr's moving on could be told with any credibility. And I think now is the right time. This is book ten, and it feels definitely like Tohr is due his healing.

It was obvious in `Lover Mine' that No'One would be taking on the difficult role of Tohr's soon-to-be chosen female. A tough task, since the Warden herself admitted that within her series universe, hellren who lose their shellan's don't mate again. They get one chance at happily-ever-after, and that's it. But the groundwork for Tohr and No'One's HEA was laid when the Warden revealed that they'd had a connection some two-hundred-years ago, before Tohr's mating to Wellsandra, back when No'One was Rosalhynda.

When `Lover Reborn' begins, No'One has been in the Brotherhood mansion for some time, but hasn't settled in. The Doggen (servants) treat her like a female of worth, but she has spent the last couple hundred years in a designated role of servitude to the Chosen females, and she's uncomfortable being the mother to a Brother's shellan, and therefore treated better than she thinks she deserves.

No'One has also found it difficult to assimilate in the Brotherhood mansion because of Tohr. She feels his anger towards her, but doesn't quite know why he's casting her glacial looks at Last Meal. Tohr, of course, sees No'One's very presence back on earth as an affront to his dead shellan. The Scribe Virgin permitted No'One to return from death - heck, the Scribe Virgin also saved Rhage's Mary from death, and bought Doc Jane back as a ghost for Vishous. But Wellsie is dead and gone. It's not fair, and it's part of the reason that he's finding it so hard to move on.

But when Lassiter comes to Tohr with the horrifying news that Wellsie is not enjoying peace in the Fade, that she is in fact stuck in the barren wasteland of in-between, Tohr is spurned into action. . . and intent on `moving on', if only to save his shellan.

Lassiter has grand plans, for Tohr and Wellsie, but also for No'One. The way Lassiter sees it, the wheel of fate started turning two-hundred-years ago when Tohr had to bury Rosalhynda beneath an apple tree after she took her own life. . .

I really liked Tohr and No'One. There's lots of talk in this book about Wellsie being in the `in-between', neither here nor there, stuck in limbo. And that's also true of Tohr and No'One. All these players are stagnate - stuck in their respective black holes. It's only when Tohr and No'One connect that they find a tether in one another, and are able to pull themselves out. . .

I liked that Tohr and No'One had a slow-burn romance. They had a lot to contend with; Tohr's grief and the fact that he's still very much in love with Wellsie. And No'One martyrdom, her thinking that she's unworthy because of her past. It is a very slow, meandering romance - but when things start to heat up, J.R. Ward brings the romance. And it's lovely and heart-warming to read Tohr getting a little slice of happiness after being miserable so long.

That's not to say Tohr and No'One's romance wasn't without fault. I, personally, think that their HEA was rushed towards the end. At one point Tohr absolutely blasts No'One - he accuses her of martyrdom and being in love with him only because she knows he won't ever love her back. He yells at her for punishing herself again and again and again, and claims that their romance isn't healthy for either of them. And, you know what?, I agreed with every single word he said. I had been thinking all those things about Tohr and No'One's HEA from the beginning - and I was head-nodding throughout Tohr's grand, vicious speech.

No'One leaves to lick her wounds, bunking with Xhex while she sorts her life out. It's during this time that she decides she won't be a martyr anymore - but if she doesn't want to be a society princess ever again, and she doesn't want to serve others, what can she do? Xhex, wisely, counsels that she'll have to figure out what she wants to do. YES! It was at this point that I despaired to see the end was so close. . . because it was at that *exact* moment, that I wanted another 200 pages. I wanted to read about No'One being by herself in the real world for a little while - going to University perhaps? Joining Marissa at the women's shelter, maybe? I wanted Tohr to take some time to accept his new level of acceptance with Wellsie's death (and even take some time seriously making amends to No'One). Instead, the Warden writes a race to the finish. . .

** Meet the Maker **

Straight up - I hated the ending. I thought the Warden wrote a total cop-out by turning to celestial hocus-pocus waffle regarding Wellsie's Fade ceremony. This isn't the first time I have rolled my eyes at the Warden writing a quick ethereal scapegoat to wrap up her book. I wasn't overly thrilled with the Scribe Virgin's quick-fix of Mary in `Lover Eternal'. And I hated the Casper magic she weaved on Doc Jane in `Lover Unbound'. The end of `Reborn' was more convenient plot scapegoating, in my opinion. And I can't help but think Tohr would have questioned, again, why No'One was bought back but his Wellsie was definitely relegated to the Fade. . . ?

So, I did like Tohr and No'One. But I felt like their relationship didn't really hit an emotional and truthful stride until the end. . . when I wanted more proof from Tohr that he was wanting to move on, and had enough room in his heart for a second shellan (and I would have really appreciated a mating ceremony on top of that!). I would have liked another season in which No'One took proper command of her life, for the first time, and spent time with her daughter figuring out who she is and what she wants to do with herself. And I would have *really* appreciated a non-sucky finale that didn't rely heavily on transcendence wackiness to explain everything.

What I didn't like about this romance, however, was made up in the form of Lassiter. He has a starring role in Tohr and No'One's romance and he is divine. Literally! I can't wait for him to get his own HEA, because hints are dropped in this novel about his one-and-only and never again love that had me raising my eyebrows. Not to mention he's so downright funny and lovely and I just want him to meet someone and fall in love!

** Band of Bastards **

I really hated `Lover Unleashed', but one good thing to come out of that book was the introduction of the Band of Bastards - a group of vampire warriors (trained by the Bloodletter) who have moved from the old country into Caldwell, with every intention of overthrowing Wrath and taking control of the glymera.

I love, love, loved the introduction of these fresh characters. I admit, beyond Tohr and Qhuay's books, I didn't quite know where the Warden would take this series next. . . but I was so happy to read so much potential in the Bastards.

There's Zypher, a handsome vampire who gives Rhage a run for his money in the `hottie' stakes. Throe was a son of nobility, now unlikely second-lieutenant in the Bastards. And their fearless leader is Xcor - a vicious fighter with scarred face and black soul who will stop at nothing to get what he wants. And on the sidelines lurks Assail, a well-to-do vampire who is moving in on Rehvenge's old drug territory, for reasons unknown. . .

The Bastards are here, and their scenes have taken over from the old lesser point-of view chapters (something I only ever skim-read!). These are some mighty-fine bad guys. . . mainly because (unlike the lessers) they each have room for redemption. I always hated the lesser POV chapters because they were just bad guys - with no wriggle room. With the Bastards, half the fun will be falling for them while they butt heads with our beloved Brothers. I can definitely envision the Warden pulling fans in all different directions with these Bastards, and I can't wait.

My only complaint with the Bastards is their similar characterization to the Brothers we've already read. Assail is a Rehvenge-type anti-hero (and many characters draw this comparison throughout `Reborn), while Zypher is another warrior cursed with good looks, like Rhage. Xcor has a scarred face like Zsadist, and a similarly mean outlook on life. I hope these are only superficial similarities - and not just regurgitated character arcs.

** The Iron Mask **

A big portion of `Reborn' is also dedicated to Xhex and John Matthew, and their trouble in paradise. John isn't coping well with Xhex's warrior status in the brotherhood, and Xhex can't envision their mating continuing as it has been. . .

I was really happy to revisit this couple, particularly because it made sense that they had a starring role in Tohr and No'One's book (Xhex being No'One's daughter, John being Tohr's son). Xhex and John definitely have unique hurdles to overcome in their mating - and it's something no other Brother has had to deal with, which makes it so interesting (and somewhat heartbreaking). These two are still great, and reading about them was a nice revisit.

** The Chosen **

Verily, I have hated Layla for a long time. I have made my feelings absolutely clear about this Mary-Sue too-perfect-to-be-likeable Chosen female. I still don't like her after reading `Reborn', but I like where her story is going. . .

Layla meets both Throe and Xcor from the Bastards - and she feeds both of them with the best of intentions (and thinking them to be members of the Brotherhood). Both Throe and Xcor are enthralled by Layla (because she's so stunningly beautiful, face like the sun. . . yadda, yadda, yadda). And Layla is equally intrigued by Xcor, a male she is oddly drawn to. . . until she learns the truth about him and the Bastards.

Thank God for the Warden - she's taking a fairly disliked character and giving her an intriguing storyline and potential HEA. I particularly like the Layla/Xcor possibility because of the Beauty and the Beast connotations. But while I like where Layla's *story* is heading, I still don't like her. In `Reborn' she again comes across like a smiling idiot whose value is only skin-deep. But you know what? With such a good story for her in the works, I want the Warden to prove my assumptions wrong. I want Layla to be totally misunderstood and more than just a pretty face - because now her story demands it.

On that note. . . I feel like I again have to say I'm a little over all the Chosen/vampire females who have been overpopulating this series. I said it in my `Lover Unleashed' review, and I'll say it again. I miss the human female love interests! I am *dying* for Rhage and Mary's short-story and I am desperate for a Brother to have a shellan who isn't vampirically beautiful, hairless and all-round perfect. I am so keen for a human female to come in who's real and relatable and has as good a romance as Mary did with Rhage. Seriously! Enough of the hairless vampire Glamazons - I want a down-to-earth human chick to get some Brotherhood booty!

** Qhuay **

Get ready to squeal because. . . it's happening! Definitely. Irrefutably. I am 99% sure that `Reborn' is the book before Qhuay. . . and, guys, it's going to be goooooood. Warden promised us bumps in their road to happily-ever-after and she delivers them in `Reborn'. Qhuinn is tangled in new family matters, and Blay is coming to terms with his never-gonna-die feelings for his best friend. Warden absolutely, positively sets these two up for a glorious HEA. Get ready, because theirs might just be the most anticipated Brotherhood book of all!

** Brotherhood **

The Warden definitely makes up for the lacklustre `Unleashed' with Tohr's `Reborn'. It was wonderful to finally read Tohr get a little slice of happy, and with a female who was equally deserving of a little bit of peace. Lassiter shines in his heavenly role, and I think after Qhuay, fans will be clamouring for his HEA. Speaking of Qhuay. . . it's freakin' happening and I couldn't be more delighted! The Band of Bastards are an intriguing lot of anti-hero's who will be devilishly delicious to read about. . . particularly for Xcor and Layla's possible HEA. `Love Reborn' is another wonderful, rip-roaringly exhilarating instalment in J.R. Ward's superb `Black Dagger Brotherhood' series.
top_profane.title: Verily good

top_profane.text:
MY COMPASS AND ANCHOR TO WINDWARD
San Francisco Book Review
4.5*

My Compass and Anchor to Windward: A Story of Love, Imagination and Adventure is the biography of Admiral Richard E. Byrd written by his granddaughter, Eleanor Byrd. This book is mainly comprised of the letters that Admiral Byrd, or Dick, as he was called by most, and his wife Marie wrote to one another during Dick’s time in the military and on private excursions.
Dick grew up in a family of three brothers. His mother, Bolling, had her hands full and Dick was the most rambunctious out of all three. One day, after being asked to bring home some groceries, all three boys come home filthy. Unbeknownst to the boys. the Byrd family is having special guests. Mrs. Ames and her two daughters, Katherine and Marie have come over to visit. From the moment eight-year-olds Dickie and Marie meet, there is a deep connection. Over the years, the Ames daughters come to visit Winchester from Boston during the summer and Dickie and Marie stay in touch the rest of the year with letters. It is this friendship that grows and grows until one day, Dick cannot hold his feelings in any longer and tells Marie that he loves her.
This book has so many wonderful parts to it. The love between Dick and Marie that grows and never falters is admirable and heartwarming. Never does Marie give up on Dick even though he leaves her and their children many times over the years on his Antarctic expeditions. With Dick’s adventurous, daredevil personality, he always wants to be out accomplishing something and has a brush with death many times, escaping it by the hair of his teeth. But this is what Marie loves about him. As Dick’s mother describes him, “He’s intelligent, ambitious, disciplined, pugnacious, and restless, but it’s all beneath that formidable veneer of charm he has…” Admiral Richard E. Byrd received twenty-two citations and special commendations, nine of which were for bravery and two for extraordinary heroism. He received over one hundred medals, served his country in two wars, and wrote five books on various topics. And as a strong, intelligent woman herself, Marie kept busy, starting her own business and raising the children at the same time, leaving behind her own legacy.
My Compass and Anchor to Windward will have readers cheering and nail-biting from chapter to chapter. Eleanor Byrd has done a beautiful job compiling the letters, timeline of history, and filing in the blanks where need be in the story of Richard and Marie Byrd. I enjoyed the many parts of the book explaining Dick’s travels, serving his country, and the pranks that he would play on his children and then his grandchildren. I also admired Marie so much for being Dick’s compass and anchor and having his back every step of the way. This book shows the importance of togetherness, family, ambition, love, and staying true to one’s self.
top_profane.title: Fabulous book

top_profane.text: There is an air of the mystical about PK Dick, possibly created by Dick's own efforts at self promotion.  Accounts of Dick given by himself and others seem to indicate that he was man who could see into his own future and see a deeper fabric of reality.  Dick was also seems to be a man who was consumed with the question of where the boundaries of his mind ended and reality began.  Dick once famously stated that 'reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away.'  The problem for PK Dick was that he seemed to not know what bits of reality to stop believing in...and perhaps in this respect, Dick is no different from the rest of us. Dick seemed to be fascinated with the Jungian psychoanalytic concept of projection: the phenomenon where aspects of perceived reality turn out to be really projections of our own minds.  Projection is a theme that appears in many of his works and it is a theme that routinely appears in this novel.  The main character Jack is repairman working on the planet Mars (or is it Outback Australia?) who has been diagnosed as schizophrenic.  Jack finds himself landed with the unwanted task of trying to develop technology to communicate with an 'autistic' child who can see into the future.  Jack is given this task by his less-then-trustworthy new boss Arnie Kott, a rich and greedy union leader.  I have heard critics say that Dick rarely wrote well-developed characters in his books, but I found the characters in Martian Time-Slip to be relatable.  All the characters in the book are both deeply flawed, but have some redeeming features, even to some extent, the scheming Arnie Kott. The theme of projection of mind is explored in relation to our protagonist Jack, but mostly in relation to the autistic boy, Manfred.  Manfred is mute, but his mind seems to be able warp time and reality and draw the minds of others into his world.  PK Dick apparently spent many hours speaking to therapists/psychiatrists about his mental ailments and I don't know if Dick was ever formally diagnosed with schizophrenia himself, but a central symptom in many people with the disorder is the broadcasting of the contents of theirs minds: their memories, perceptions and thoughts, out into the world and into the minds of others. Hence, Martian Time-Slip is a little peek into what the world would be like if the schizophrenic's mind was not delusional but an accurate perception of  what "reality" is or how reality "works."  An interesting question that perhaps from time-to-time all of us may at times ponder.  Dick's novel is a good read, it has a well contained plot touching on some interesting ideas.  An speculative side-note: the novel explores the idea that the autistic Manfred lives in a world where his conscious sense of time is different from other people, hence the attempt, portrayed in the novel, to produce a machine that will alter the temporal frequency of sensory experiences in order to communicate with Manfred.  Forty-plus years later, some academic researchers have suggested that schizophrenia, or at least some aspects or symptoms of schizophrenia, could be due to altered timing or calibration between between sensory inputs to the brain and motor actions or movements of the body, suggesting that perhaps, for at least some schizophrenics, their mind/body has 'slipped' out of time with the rest of the world.  Did PK Dick pre-cognise future cognitive neuroscience theories of schizophrenia back in 1966?  It's at least fun to suppose he did.
top_profane.title: A Novel About the Nature of Reality, Social Politics and Purchasing Property.

top_profane.text:
TRIGGERS: rape, incest, pedophilia

Thankfully, I did not have to spend money on this book. I received it through the Kindle First program.

It took far more alcohol than I want to admit to get through that book.

The story had potential, true, but it had potential in the way that Jane Eyre had potential over 150 years ago – except Jane Eyre lived up to it’s potential.

Miramont’s Ghost, essentially, failed.

Let’s start with the most glaring issue. Yeah, it may be a spoiler, but I’d rather “spoil” this than have some unsuspecting reader stumble upon this with no advance warning: the rape. By her cousin. After remembering some kind of pre-molestation behavior from when she was a child.

Fine, I get it. Something had to send her over the edge. Then again, the way she was going, realizing what was being done to her, maybe not. Maybe the rape was as completely, utterly pointless as it seemed. Not even a claim of being historically “accurate” would fly by me right now. The girl’s life was completely and utterly dreary. We get it.

No, really, we do. Absolutely HORRIBLE.

There’s absolutely nothing noteworthy about this book, aside from some glaringly inaccurate statements about things that make me really question how much the author cared about being “accurate” aside from the “history.” Even in the end, all we’re treated to is some ridiculously long and pointless diatribe about how she’s trapped and the ones who perpetuated her misery are gone and escaped, and, wait, oh my god, a revelation! She can let go! She’s free! She’s no longer a ghost!

To Summarize:

Adrienne, our heroine, is clairvoyant (which also has absolutely nothing to do with the story except to possibly be the reason for all her misery). Her grandfather, governess and eventually a man named Gerard are the only people who really care about her. Her aunt is suspicious of her and wants her to stay quiet. Her mother is spineless and her father is an adulterous bastard.

Adrienne looses her grandfather, looses her governess thanks to said adulterous bastard, looses Gerard thanks to scheming bitch of an aunt. Then bitch aunt tells spineless mother that she’s taking Adrienne to the US to live with her and her son, the pedophile priest who can do no wrong in the eyes of his mother.

Bitch aunt tells rest of the family Adrienne drowned on the trip. She’s trapped. She’s brought to Miramont under the guise of bitch aunties maid, and is treated as such. She is given a room in servants quarters. She cleans, tends to the bitch aunt, all while hoping her love Gerard will come save her or plotting an escape.

Of course, her trying to watch pedophile priest/cousin gives him the wrong idea. After bitch aunt increases suffering of Adrienne intentionally, dear cousin takes it upon himself to act on delusions he’s created around his “whore cousin” constantly watching him.

Eventually Adrienne tries to kill herself and succeeds, after which bitch aunt returns to France and pedophile priest has to flee due to his pedophile’s ways leaking to the general public. But not after sealing Adrienne’s body away since the ground was too hard to dig. *snorts*

Then we reach the end, where we receive the aforementioned pointless diatribe to a ridiculously unsatisfying ending. In fact, the whole damn book was one of the least satisfying I’d ever read, and isn’t worth the energy to say anything more about it.
top_profane.title: Trigger warnings!

top_profane.text:
Get Rich or Get Lucky by Max Nowaz is about Adam the protagonist who purchases a dilapidated house that he is renovating. He comes across a German book that has spells. As he tries one to his surprise it worked. Adam invites his friends Charlie and Dick to show them. Charlie and Dick laugh at Adam as they think the whole thing is just silly. Dick tries a spell and nothing happens. When Adam does the same spell on Dick he turns into a Crayfish. After a spell is used from the book the sky rains which results the loss of Dick the Crayfish who was being held in the pond with 3 other Crayfish.

Adam tries to turn Dick back but then his house is burgled and the book goes missing. Rachael, Dick’s wife comes looking for him. Adam plays it cool. Who would believe him anyway? Adam has several people coming to the house to help fix it up, in particular is the roofer Daley who seems to act suspicious after Adam’s house was burgled. What about the nosey neighbors who always seem to know what is going on but actually misses the strange goings on at Adams house.

When Rachael reports Dick missing, Charlie gets questioned and Adam becomes the polices main interest. With Dick’s disappearance life seems to be looking up for Charlie. Charlie walks with a sense of confidence while the ladies are drawn to him but he keeps coming back to the notion he needs to get the book back and find a way to bring Dick back.

I found this book to have a steady semi slow pace, but started to pick quite rapidly about 75% in. I absolutely loved the ending! I recommend Get Rich or Get Lucky to those who enjoy a semi realistic but fantasy style novel. There is a lot of drama but a sense of humor, particularly to Adam.
top_profane.title: Worth the read

top_profane.text:
Dick by R.C. Stephens is the first book I have read by her and I loved it. This is not your traditional story of love where the boy and girl meet, fall in love, get married and have kids. Dixon or as his friends call him "Dick" has earned the reputation of just that. He sleeps around, a lot. Dick also has two children whom he adores and loves more than anything. He is really involved in their lives at school and at home. Dick has made is way around the woman at his children's school and every woman tries to bed Dick. At a gala Dick meets a woman that intrigues him but finds out she is married. One morning he drops his kids off and his little boy wants him to talk to the teacher, who happens to be the woman that has plagued his thoughts since the gala, Eden.

Eden is in a loveless marriage and has a son. She is a teacher and loves it. She also has heard the rumors about Dick. Eden has secrets and now she needs help and she calls Dick for assistance. Leaving a loveless marriage is not going to be easy. Falling for Dick shouldn't be either. To complicate matters she finds out another secret and has to make a choice. Will she choose her happily ever after with Dick, and can he truly change his ways for her?

This is so much more than a love story, this is about realizing people that love you will do anything for you and you just need to trust in them. I would recommend this book and look forward to reading more from R.C. Stephens.
top_profane.title: Not a Traditional Love Story

top_profane.text:
Enjoyed this series. Cried from time to time in the books preceding this one. But this one just pissed me off and turned me off to this author. Was Allison blown up on purpose? Was Jane mind controlled to insist Allison get her "special fix"? How could Allison resent Jane "causing" her injury if it was all a set-up since Allison had been "recruited" from the beginning? And now here's Allison at the bitter end betraying Jane because of "mind control" but she wasn't "mind controlled" when she conspired against everything Jane was working for and towards? How is Allison NOT responsible for Dan's death? Killing off Jane's husband, the one person that had her back out of love, was a crap ending. 100% crap. This last one took a pretty solid series and muddied up good guys, bad guys, and far too many straddling that fence in such a confusing back and forth of crap.....the senate hearing was crap. Candice and her tangled web? More crap.....she knew it was going to go bad and hid herself letting people who she was supposed to be on the same team with be murdered?? With Cynthia neck deep in both sides being played as well?? Crap. Crap. Crap. So at the end Jane had her dogs and her kids, now minus their dad. But nope, the kids didn't matter either....fostered and I guess newly adopted kids packed off after the funeral without a bit of hesitation for their needs or grief. Why they were even introduced into the story to be fostered/adopted is a complete mystery now as they were tossed aside as, well, crap would be. I actually had kinder thoughts regarding the finale of Game of Thrones on tv than I do for this series. My advice is don't bother, don't waste your time plowing through 7 books of dog deaths, turmoil, angst for Jane, and harping on her "neck down" circle for it to boil down to betrayal, senseless death, and humanity at its worst. Honestly, Jane should have shot her dogs then herself and let her sister in law have her kids. That would have at least rounded out the betrayal of this last book with an ending that made some sense. Especially rather than the weak promise of Jane teamed up with everyone that betrayed her doing dog show trials as her weak cover for covert missions with people she can't trust.
My advice is read Game of Thrones instead, it will end making more sense and be far more satisfying.
top_profane.title: Won't read this author again

top_profane.text:
Dixon & Eden...

I was so looking forward to this book! And I was NOT disappointed!!!! LOVED!!!!

I mean when you are seriously HOT, Rich, A fantastic Dad, and have 10” in ur pants…..whats not to love??

I’ve not read this author before but I am now a HUGE fan. The story was just amazing and the writing fantastic. The characters are so deep and flawed but also amazing and heartwarming. **sigh**

Dixon has an alter ego….Dick. He is a man-whore/Dick. He’s kind of an asshole/Dick. He’s packing in his pants/Dick. And there’s the whole play on words….Dix/Dick.

But deep down he’s just Dixon/Amazing. He’s a great guy. Loving and caring and just adores his kids. He was married and loved married life. She burned him and on top of his awful childhood...he’s not been the same person since.

Eden is in a terrible marriage in which she only stays for her son. She’s a kindergarten teacher and just the sweetest most down to earth person you could ever hope to meet. She’s also very unhappy. At twenty-eight years old she feels eighty-eight. She rarely goes out and her husband ignores her completely. They only have sex like once a year! And she seems find with it. Until….she runs into Dick at a presidential dinner with her husband-Dick is enamored and Eden’s….remembering.

They know each other from their childhoods and there is something there from before. She realizes this way before he does. She’s also married, which doesn’t matter to him too much, but she doesn’t want to cheat on her husband. But boy does Dick bring out the woman in her!! This sweet woman is pure lust in bed!! Hawt!!!

They find each other again at his child’s school. She just happens to be his son’s kindergarten teacher!! All sorts of things happen to these two people that push and pull them in their “relationship”. The way this author weaves the heaviness of marriage and divorce with the heaviness of overcoming childhood to the happiness of finding love again and realizing family doesn’t have to be blood and people do love you.

I can’t say enough good things about this book! LOVED!!!! Can’t wait to read more by this author!!!

Highly Recommend!!!
top_profane.title: LOVED!!!! Amazing!!!!!! Must Read!!!!

top_rant.text:
Current DLS:
Stolen Melissa's paper/ Nominated for a Golden Orchid with stolen paper/ Has a weird memory about Ali, which she isn't sure about/Melissa finds out and so do her parents

Spencer: She is talking to a therapist about her life and actually enjoys talking to Dr. Evans a lot. When she comes out of the office, though, her mom tells her that one of her papers are up for this great literary award. Spence feels terrible, because it was Melissa's stolen paper that was nominated and it looks like Melissa knows about that, too! Spence is feeling more and more frustrated as people continue to congratulate her on being nominated. She starts to think that the Rosewood Stalker is `A'. Now, Spencer is freaking out, because she thinks Melissa knows! Spence really did the right thing. When Melissa was being a competitive Bitch with a capital B, Spencer just said she was right and totally shocked/confused Melissa. Gosh, what sweet revenge! After the yearbook meeting during lunch, Spencer looked at her phone and the unopened text from `A' was still there. The night that Ali went missing, Spencer went after her, but she cannot really remember what happened after. It was like a piece of that night is missing from her memory. She has just remembered a memory of Ali laughing nastily and turning away. I wonder if its from that night. Spence's stolen paper has made it to the finals in the Golden Orchid. She suspects that Andrew knows about the stolen paper, because he was asking a lot of suspicious questions about the sources that she had used. Andrew is being creepy watching Spencer's every move like that. `A' wants Spencer to do something for her or else `A' will tell everyone about the stolen paper. `A' is trying to tell Spence that the murderer is herself, but I don't think she gets it yet and I don't think that Spence is the murderer. Spencer is getting little snippets of a lost memory and it seems to suggest that Ali and her were arguing about something. Her family is currently doing the interview for the newspaper about her winning the Golden Orchid. Spence is going to be on the front cover of the paper and `A' isn't sure whether she should tell the whole world or not. Spencer really is over her head in all this mess and Melissa seems to know things that she shouldn't. Spence has gone to see Doctor Evans again. The doctor wants to try to hypnotize her to get the suppressed memory out of her. The memory comes out and its about a fight that her and Ali had on the night that Ali went missing, but Spencer cannot remember the last thing that Ali said to her. Spencer caught someone watching her behind a truck tire. This series is really starting to get creepy! Spencer and her parents are out to dinner to celebrate Spencer's accomplishments. Her parents talk to her about the doctor that she has been seeing and asks her if she feels much better after going to her. Their waiter brings up the Rosewood Stalker, which I'm pretty sure is `A', who I am pretty sure is Mona. Spence's mom tells her about the time a man tried to rob her at gun point with her and Melissa right there with her. Her mom told her that that was the one time that Spencer has had amnesia and Spence sitting there still could not remember that day. Then, all of a sudden, Spencer had a fever and her parents had to miss Melissa's national spelling bee contest. To make sure that Spencer would be okay, Melissa missed many things so that her parents could stay close to Spencer. I think that is the stem of the problem Spencer has with Melissa. After all those years, Melissa hasn't gotten over being second place to Spencer, so she made Spencer feel that way. Oh, boy! I'm glad that I don't have a sister! Spencer is afraid of herself, since now she is convinced that that wasn't her last blackout. While tending her mother's garden, Ian Thomas came over to see Melissa, but stops to talk/flirt with Spencer. Oh, Snap! Melissa told Ian about her therapy sessions and that she had heard Spencer screaming. While in the hot tub, her sister climbs in with her and tells her that she's mad at Dr. Evans. Melissa tells her that the doctor told her that she chooses untrustworthy guys. She thinks that she knows who Ian was cheating with, while they were dating in high school. Spencer asks her what she would do to the girl who cheated with Ian and Melissa says that she might have done something already to her. What if it was Ali? What if Melissa killed Ali? Spencer is getting ready for Mona's party. Her parents show her the front page of the newspaper with her picture on it and that the story is up online.

Current DLS:
Father is living with girlfriend/ Mom kicked her out of the house/Mick isn't speaking to her and neither is her mom/ Tug-of-war between Ezra and Sean

Aria: Is still super upset with Spencer over her lying about Toby after all those years of them being friends. Her mother is still not close to her, but at least she is talking to her. Her father is living with his girlfriend. Her thing with Ezra is over especially since she found out that he had a girlfriend. Hanna is still mad at her for going out with Sean, which she is still doing. Aria ran into Ezra while waiting for Sean to come back to his car to give her a ride home. Ezra handed her an essay on The Scarlet Letter talking about if adultery was okay in some circumstances. She took it, but said that it wasn't ever acceptable. Sean was driving her home, when her dad texted her to talk to him at Hollis. She gets this crazy idea in her head as Sean dropped her off at Hollis. She walked right up to Meredith and painted a big, red A on her dress and stormed out.
After, she had a crazy laugh and felt very satisfied. Warns Em in a voice message that she should talk to her before doing anything, because `A' is capable of really anything. He says tours go through the house everyday and that she works hard to keep everything clean and neat. They cross Ali's grave that Aria didn't know about. They stopped and she thought of the things she wanted to say to her, but couldn't say out loud. They go to leave and Sean makes up a couple game for them. Rabbit. KISS! Aria thinks its stupid. She hangs up on Sean before answering his question if she was going to Mona's. She arrives at Ezra's and they drink Scotch together. They talk about their families. Hanna comes home to change clothes and go out to have dinner with Mona, but finds out that she's under investigation. All her old friends are at her house with the police out back. When she walks through the house, her mother tells her that she doesn't have to volunteer at the burn clinic anymore, which leaves Hanna surprised. She is still upset that her father doesn't want to speak/hear/see her anymore. Someone leaked out a video to the press with the girls talking about text-messages. The video was recorded back when Ali went missing. The police think that Alison's killer is still in Rosewood that the person lives in Rosewood and has been since the beginning. Mona calls and is extremely upset to find out that Hanna is with her old friends, but the officer takes her phone before she could really explain to Mona what was going on. The officer brings up Toby's suicide and asks the girls what was Ali's secret. The girls all get a message from `A' saying not to tell anyone about her. While at the mall waiting for Mona to call about her dress fitting, Hanna finds Mona with the two mean girls from school. Mon and Hanna get into a mock-fight. Mona said that the court was going to be bigger, because small courts are boring. She tries to talk to Mona alone, but Mona said that they were late for the fitting. Hanna was about to follow Mona, when her purse spilled open. As she picks up her stuff, Hanna runs into an old guy friend from school named Lucas. Hanna tells him she has to go, but Lucas tells her that she has to pay the bill, which she does. Hanna gets a text from `A' telling her that one of her old friends was hiding something from her and that Mona wasn't her friend at all. Hanna is waiting for Mona at their lunch table to give her her birthday present. As she's waiting for Mona, Hanna goes through some old texts from Ali. Hanna's gift turns horribly wrong when the plane in the sky spelled, "Get ready to fart with Mona." instead of "Get ready to party with Mona." To top it all off, the two mean girls got a hip pop star to play at Mona's party. NOW, Hanna is officially uninvited to Mona's party. I know who did it, `A', which is Mona. Probably exactly how she wanted it to happen. Bitch. Everyone is talking about Hanna and how she's now a big loser. Poor Hanna! Looks like `A' was right, Mona isn't really her friend. After all, she is `A'. Hanna sees Mona and confronts her about the rumors about her going around. Mona says that she's only said true things about her, which means that she has been spreading rumors about her. Mona just called her a loser. Bitch. Hanna just thought of something interesting. Mona doesn't feel comfortable changing in front of others. I wonder why? Could she have had some surgery to make herself like Ali? Hanna is accidentally in the boy's bathroom and she's crying in front of Lucas, a loser of a guy! She is actually going to go somewhere with Lucas tomorrow as friends... Wow, I think Hanna might actually end up liking him more than a friend. (Then, her descent to loserdom would be complete.) YES! I KNOW IT! When Hanna saw Mona leaving the clinic that day is because she had lipo done there! But, why if Mona is `A' would she reveal something like that? Maybe Mona isn't `A'. Hanna is with Lucas at a park and she is wondering what kind of a surprise could Lucas be giving her. Hanna isn't sure whether she should spread the rumor about Mona around, because it felt different to do it to Mona, who was practically her best friend maybe even her only since Ali disappeared. YAY! The surprise is Hanna and Lucas are going to go up in a rainbow striped hot air balloon! Lucas told Hanna about his suicidal brother and how keeping busy keeps him sane, when Hanna admitted that she eats cheese-based snacks and then throws them up to keep sane! I cannot believe that she just told him that and neither can she! She just told Lucas about `A'! I wonder what's going to happen now!?! Hanna decided to tell Lucas everything and he still likes her. He says that he's glad that she isn't Alison, because he never liked her after she started a rumor about him in the 7th grade. Lucas says he likes her!!! She agrees to Lucas's invitation to his antiparty to Mona's birthday party. Hanna buys a new blackberry, because she refuses to think that everyone would drop her that fast because of the fight with Mona. For once, she wishes that `A' would text her. So, she buys a new phone thinking that her old one is broken. Mona walked into T-Mobil just as the guy behind the counter is having a hard time hooking her up with a new phone. Hanna tells Mona about the message on her garage roof and she apologizes to Mona about everything. Mona tells her that maybe things will go back to the way they were again and Hanna wonders what that maybe means. When Hanna goes home, her mom tell her that a package arrived for her. The package was her court dress to Mona's party. She thinks that must mean that she is re-invited. She decides to cancel her little party with Lucas. Hanna's court dress is really tight and I think Mona wanted it that way to make Hanna look really fat. Mona had changed the court dress on her and Mona was wearing the same dress as her. Mona told her that she changed the dress than she called Hanna a whale. `A' had sent Hanna the dress and she fell. The dress slit open and Mona and the court girls just let it happen and turned their back on her. Hanna tells the others about Mona's lipo, but no one seems to believe her. The big t.v. on screen showed everything that happened in replay. Lucas came to the rescue, but how did he know to come? Lucas gave Hanna a sweatshirt and a pair of shorts. She starts to cry in Lucas's car. She ended up on Lucas's lap and they started kissing. He ended up on top of her and touching her stomach and took his shirt off. They laid together like that for awhile, then someone was trying to come into the room. Hanna thought it was `A'. An old man ended up coming into the room. They left and Lucas walked her back to Mona's party to get her car and he said that he would call her tomorrow. As Hanna's driving away, she getts a text from `A' saying that she guessed that it wasn't lipo Mona got and to not believe everything that she hears. `A''s number wasn't unknown caller. It was an actual number and Hanna knows exactly who it is. Hanna waits in the parking lot for the other girls to arrive. She sees a strange car and then she sees Aria and Emily's car. She started running towards their car, when Aria shouted at her to look out. The SUV was going straight towards Hanna. Hanna was hit by the SUV!!!!!!!OMG!!!!

Current DLS:
Feels guilty about Toby's death/ being with Maya/Parents have forced her into the Tree Tops program/Rosewood Day's NEW It Girl/

Emily: Still feels really guilty for Toby's death. She feels like she was responsible for it. Her mom keeps on leaving her self-help books to help her deal with Toby's death. Her mom thought that Toby was her boyfriend, but he wasn't officially. Em is seeing Maya secretly and we all know that with `A' around a secret isn't a secret for long. Maya wants them to be more open about it, but Em wants to take it slow. Hears about the Rosewood Stalker, which is basically a peeping Tom. Someone is trying to gain information from people by peeping. Em has decided to tell the police about `A', even though `A' had told them not too. Her sister and fellow teammates are acting really weird at the swim meet. People in the stands were reading the heat sheet too interestingly. Two boys taunted Em and Mona personally invited her to her birthday party, which was very weird because none of these things ever happens to her. Everyone knows about the kiss between her and Maya. The photo is everywhere of them two kissing. Aria comes up to her telling her that they need to talk. `A' sent Em a text saying that since she was going to out `A', `A' was going to out her, which `A' did.

A: I think that Mona could be `A'. Don't ask why, but I think she could be. Ali teased her for years and so did the others. Why wouldn't she want revenge? Mona also always seems to be around, when things go wrong. When they were young, they spent time avoiding Mona. She was always walking across the street or trying to hang with them. I think stalking them and watching them from afar made her feel apart of their group. I think that she is most definitely `A'. I mean, why else would she be so cool with all of Hanna's problems and freak out when she finds out that Hanna has been keeping secrets from her. Especially since now, they are practically BFFs. The way Mona reacted to Hanna being with the others also adds to my suspicion. OMG! I think Mona was sending threatening messages to Ali, before she was murdered! Maybe, she even murdered Ali! Although a text from `A' says different, I think that she did kill Ali. But, just maybe, Mona isn't `A'. Last message from `A': She knew too much. - A
top_rant.title: Mona?

top_rant.text:
After finishing Stephanie Ahn's debut and Book 1 of her Harrietta Lee Series, "Deadline," literally in a blink of an eye (novella length only), that left me wanting more (Harry's ongoing adventures plus a certain demon!), I had to read Book 2 right away! Plus, I was dead curious about Ahn's sophomore writing and storytelling skills. Needless to say, I devoured it at top speed and came out of it totally and completely entertained, satiated, satisfied, and more inspired and thrilled than ever! Ahn is most definitely a talented writer and tale-spinner of the wildest, most daring, uninhibited and imaginative kind in the world of urban fantasy! 

"Bloodbath." Yes, that's the word Ahn used to title the 2nd installment of her Harrietta Lee series. And yes, it means what it means, literally and figuratively, and she made no qualms about it, whatsoever, in bringing that title to life in weaving the sequel to Harry's adventures as a disgraced blood witch earning her keep as an "unofficial" P.I. in the streets of New York! Ahn definitely nailed the "sequel" formula perfectly, imo: BIGGER. BETTER. MORE. Much, much more.

The first thing I noticed when I started the book was, it read very differently than Book 1. Whilst the format of Ahn's narrative remained the same - real-time first-person - the style was decidedly more structured and controlled, the rhythm and flow more settled, with a darker and more serious atmosphere, stakes were higher because the plot was much more intricate with complex implications and more far-reaching effects. Another thing I noticed was the setting/set up. As I read on, there was a distinct flavour of a Harry Potter/Fantastic Beasts influence specifically in the Ministry of Magic/Magical Congress of the USA realm and all it represented in the magical world. For Ahn's Harry and her world, it was called the "Council" which, like MoM/MACUSA, governed every magical creature living amongst the human world. In this case, it's more like MACUSA since Harry's world is in America. The organisational structure was set up just like any multi-national company, with the International Council as the parent company, with regional councils governing assigned areas. The featured Council in Harry's world (New York) was called the Northeastern American Regional Council. Wicked, innit? Can you imagine the potential for story expansion, what with the existence of other regional councils, inside and outside of America, and the International Council headed by this neutral judge and tie-breaker called the Arbiter? I simply LOVE it! 

Now with the existence of a "government" (the Council) in the mage (magical) world, obviously there would be politics, power play, power struggle, rules governing the mages (magic folk) in conjunction with the non-mage (ala "No-Mag" in FB, eh?) world. In this installment, Ahn tossed us right into the Council's world. She had given us a brief intro about the NARC (gosh, doesn't cast a positive light on the Council with THAT acronym, does it? Bloody cheek, Ms. Ahn!) in Book 1, but in this sequel, she opened the door a bit wider allowing us to take a closer look at the Council - the who, the what, the how - which thrilled me to bits because ever since Book 1, I really wanted to discover more about the "all-powerful" entity. Albeit Ahn didn't reveal a whole lot but enough to whet my appetite and hope for more to come in future installments! 

This sequel picked up a week after the end of "Deadline." What started off as a simple missing persons case for Harry, turned out to be much more than that when her BFF, Joy, was discovered missing and reported by Joy's client-turned-close mate, Nikki. From then on, everything just spiralled out of control, the deeper Harry delved into in her investigation. I won't divulge anything else about the plot because it needs to be explored directly by the reader in order to feel its significance, implications and effects. All I can say is, Ahn cleverly weaved a simple web that quickly mutated into something bigger, darker, wilder, with twists and turns that kept me on my toes and my adrenaline level pumping right until the end with her deliberate pacing from (relatively) calm waters in the first act, to rising waves in the second, that broke into a full-blown tsunami in the final one! 

With a bigger more intricate plot meant more characters, obvs. Speaking of, I was dead chuffed anticipating what Ahn had in-store for my favourite character, Lilith, the adorable, mischievous and mysterious demon, in this installment! And I was bowled over with excitement that Lilith truly put a solid stamp in Harry's world in this story. Forget about her relationship with Harry for a mo, Lilith's back story was still largely shrouded in mystery even though Ahn revealed a little more about her in the context of her "contract" with Harry. But Ahn's very subtle description and portrayal of Lilith throughout this book were very nuanced that carried a deeper meaning, albeit unspoken. Whether it was a deliberate move by Ahn to create a deeper layer of mystery and intrigue with Lilith's character via a series of richly expressed non-verbal body language, I thought it was brilliantly executed. Lilith was already an enigma since Book 1. With added nuances simmering beneath her character in this story, I'm even more curious about Lilith's true character and her purpose beyond the confines of her relationship with Harry. I'd love to explore her back story especially after a certain reveal toward the end of the book. Now, back to her relationship with Harry, I utterly LOVED Ahn's depiction of their chemistry together, this implicit bond they had (ever since Book 1, really!) - Lilith's dominant behaviour to Harry's submissive - was utterly captivating, borderline intoxicating to witness, what with that undeniable magnetic pull of connecting energy like an invisible thread that was binding them closer together at each passing time, much to their exasperation, which interestingly, the reluctance came more from Lilith's end than Harry's. Who was Lilith, really? What was her purpose with Harry? Why did she seem more unsettled and perplexed every time she felt something? Why that secrecy in her role as a demon in the demon world? So many questions. So much intrigue. Maybe I'm just reaching but I have a feeling that as Ahn expands the series, Lilith's role will slowly reveal its true purpose. Well, that's my wishful thinking, anyway! In any case, Ahn has created a richly layered, deeply empathetic character in a demon that I'm completely enamoured of!

Apart from Lilith returning as part of the cast, Luce, Harry's coven sister, was back, too, albeit briefly but the significance of her role as a healer was defined and delved into a little more in this story. I do hope to find out more about her in future installments. As for new characters, I loved every one of them, particularly Nikki, a client-turned-good friend of Joy's, who quickly bonded with Harry in the wake of Joy's disappearance. Just when I thought Ahn had created an intriguing character in Nikki, she pulled a rabbit out of the hat about this unassuming character that I was simply gobsmacked! Bloody brilliant is all I'm gonna say about it! You simply have to read it to find out! And then, there was Harry's computer/tech geek of a close mate, Izzy (Isabella)!! Another fantastic character from Ahn's brilliant mind! Two words came to mind when I explored these two characters: Unique. Diverse. In every sense. 'Nuff said. And then there were others, namely one of the Council Enforcers whose name I won't disclose because the reveal was funny, who had amusing chemistry with our adorably idiotic Harry! Then Dick...omg, what a perfect name for him because he was a bloody idiot of a knob, and then Junhyun, the son of Harry's neighbour....Ooo... All I can say is, Ahn is a bloody creative storyteller! Yeh, the outside-the-box thinking kind! Well done, Ms. Ahn! Even the antagonists were utterly intriguing to me who were weaved into the story ever-so brilliantly. Even that true muppet of a vamp! Blimey! Never read a vamp with THAT kind of nutter juice in them! Fan-bloody-tastic! There was one character, the Arbiter, who immediately piqued my curiosity because of a certain reveal which I'm hoping is a breadcrumb that Ahn deliberately threw in this story for future references. Hmm.... 

As for Harry, I loved how Ahn shaped her character so far. I really enjoyed exploring her dilemmas, her struggle to block out her emotional feelings, whether it was about her mentor, her family, her missing friend or more crucially, about Lilith, all of which Ahn had deftly depicted in Harry's character development, with resonating effect. Harry's ongoing struggle with her self-worth (her constant self-deprecation, for starters!) that Ahn ever-so subtly injected into Harry's character as she continued with her journey of loss, contemplation, guilt, as she tried to reconcile, let go, accept and hopefully later on, embrace love. There was no romance in this book, just like the previous installment, but subtle nuances were there. That's what I love about Harry and Lilith's relationship. There was nothing deeply concrete yet but there was certainly a deliberate slow-burn of sorts in terms of how these two polar opposites, yet, somehow shared an affinity, viewed their relationship. Their interactions that were mostly superficial with loads of teasing and sarcasm and dry wit usually from Lilith's end with defensive and funny reactions from Harry, which always fell flat, much to her dismay, that only invigorated Lilith's gleefully playful condescension! For e.g....

Lilith stands up on her tiptoes—or rather, stands up even higher on her toes, since she’s got those fancy digitigrade cat feet—and pats the top of my head. “It’s okay, Witchy, it’s not your fault you’re stupid,” she consoles in a voice that’s entirely too charitable for her smug face.

GUFFAW! 

But at certain moments, with Lilith's abrupt change of behaviour when she thought things were on the edge of being a little serious between them, they would then turned quietly contemplative but laced with emotional resonance. Also, the fact that Lilith was a demon so she had to adhere to some strict rules within the demon world provided another layer of complexity as far as they were concerned. To see how Lilith manoeuvre her way around them when it came to Harry was utterly enthralling. This relationship between Harry and Lilith, is one that must only be tackled using the slow-burn approach, which Ahn had skilfully weaved the layers that were then slowly peeled off little by little at every stage, with intoxicating flair. Obviously, there are still many hidden layers left unpeeled, if what was included in this installment is any indication. I do hope it spans the entire series, how ever many installments Ahn is coming up with. At this point, after reading 2 installments, I can see at least 5 to 7 books in total because of how Ahn has laid out the canvas depicting Harry's world. So many possibilities, so much potential, because she's opened up many angles already in the form of the Council and the others including the International Council, the Arbiter, Harry's family past, Lilith, Luce's story, the demon world, even other possibilities, i.e. Izzy and Nikki, other connected magical creatures, the possibilities are just endless at this point, really. Just like J.K. Rowling's Fantastic Beasts Series. Which makes me wonder if Ahn, like Rowling, has the entire Harrietta Lee series planned out in a certain number of installments already. Christ, I do hope so! Makes my level of anticipation even more acute! 

Back to the plot, without divulging anything besides the aforementioned, let's just say that whilst I was devouring every bit of adventure, the thrills, those twists and turns, the suspense, and the action, there were moments that reminded me of, amongst other things, Harry Potter's pensieve sequence, and *gasp* Stephen King's "Doctor Sleep," which, speaking of, I had JUST finished reading before I delved into this book! So imagine my utter aghast at a certain direction of the plotline where I was like, "Whatthebloodyhell?!" The parallel was just uncanny especially right after reading one and then the other. I'm sure if I hadn't read King's book right before this one, I wouldn't have freaked out like I did! Yup, what are the odds, eh? But there you go. And oh, the descriptions of the violent action sequences, the action moves, Harry's "OMG, NO!" impossibly harrowing "physical exertions" (alternative mild definition used here, mind!), and desperate predicament(s) were so vividly detailed I could feel every sensation! And Lilith! Oh, Lilith!!! As if I couldn't loved her character more, Ahn splayed even more honest-to-goodness angel dust of preciousness into this endearing, heart-tugging demon that I just...can't..!!! Ohhhhh!!!! Talk about an oxymoronic character, eh? Simply MARVELLOUS!!! Bloody well, done, Ms. Ahn! I bow to you, oh great one! I want MORE Lilith, please!!! Oops! Pardon me! Got a bit mad there for a mo! Needless to say, all the more reason to read this book is all I have to say about it. 

There were two memorable moments that absolutely struck me emotionally. One was the unbelievably suspenseful sequence in the final act that involved Lilith's heartbreaking anguish and her desperate moves, and later on, Harry's quieter, more contemplative action related to Lilith.... I can't say more because that scene needs to be read in order to capture that seemingly nonchalant but tethered with so much emotion that Harry herself couldn't quite comprehend but we as readers would feel. And that's why their relationship needs to be at a slow-burning pace. Let it simmer, let the flame grow leisurely throughout the passage of time because the reward at the end of it would be infinitely more enduring and memorable, you know, like Ron and Hermione, than otherwise depicted.

Then, there was the quiet, sombre and deeply affecting moment in the bath tub scene. The unspoken, the silence between Harry and Lilith with eyes that spoke volumes in their magnetic pull toward each other. They say that the eyes are a window to the soul. No truer words to describe what Harry and Lilith shared there and then, in that moment frozen in time. That heavy air of a certain feeling and emotion between them seemed to scare Lilith, which Ahn depicted with such subtlety but packed with emotional resonance! At least for me! I reckon when you get to that scene, you'll know what I'm getting at. 

There were several interactions especially toward the end between Lilith and Harry that only further deepened my utter adoration for them as mates, partners-in-crime, and hopefully something more. The one that really stuck was this exchange (only a snippet because...spoilers):

[Lilith] “Witchy, when did you forget that I’m a demon? ...I told you before. I’m a demon, not your personal Supergirl. I’m in this to protect my own interests. Don’t count on me to do anything else.”

[Harry] Normally, she’d say this kind of thing with an airy nonchalance. Right now, her voice is just hollow. Her body is tense against my back, and I’m haunted by shadows of the words she’s not saying. Or is that just the famous Hellish manipulation? She sounds genuine, but… I can’t be sure. You can never be sure with a demon.

The reprise of Lilith's "I'm a demon, not your personal Supergirl" reverberated throughout the book with affecting fervour. At least for me. 

All in all, I absolutely, positively LOVED this installment! Ahn has truly created an incredible series, with only 2 books in, deliberately dropping breadcrumbs at strategic points in every installment, effectively laying out her own "yellow brick road" for Harry & Co. and us, readers, to tread on and explore. The subtle breadcrumbs mean there's a larger plot at play in Harry's universe. There's just so much potential to explore so I hope Ahn has planned out the entire series with a blazing end - like how Rowling has mapped out her entire Fantastic Beasts Series. I loved the fact that Rowling announced firmly that the story would span 5 books, much like Harry Potter with 7 books, that's it. That's what I love to see in serials. A fantastically weaved saga with a finite end. I'm hoping Ahn's going the Rowling direction. *crossing fingers in anticipation*

If you haven't realised my view about this book already, "Bloodbath" is a RESOUNDING MUST-READ! An ENTHUSIASTIC Recommendation from me! And, please, if you haven't read Book 1, "Deadline," yet, I IMPLORE you to do so before you read this one. You'll not regret it especially if you're a fan of witches and demons, ghosts and psychics, fae and the likes, Harry Potter and Fantastic Beasts, ala the urban fantasy genre in either general (mainstream) fiction or lesbian fiction! But what makes Ahn's "Harrietta Lee Series" special is that it's Lesbian Fiction/LGBT Fiction with a multitude of diverse characters, be it race, size, physicality, sexuality or the likes, being represented. How wicked is that?! Simply marvellous!

With a knowledge that Book 3, "Flytrap" is coming out in 2020/21, I'm utterly chuffed about it! Psyching myself in anticipation, tbh!! Books 1 and 2 are definitely on my list of reread material! Ahn is undoubtedly on my list of newly discovered authors to watch now and will be looking out for all her future lesfic writing endeavours, that's for sure! What a young talent of a writer, indeed!

Well done, Ms. Ahn, and thank you for this rich, diverse, exciting, fun, always hilarious, compelling, utterly addictive series which I hope will end in all its glory with a few books to its collection that I will cherish like I do with HP, only this time, it's lesfic! Wicked! And... for gifting me Lilith, the infinitely cheeky, mischievous and totally endearing demon with a heart of gold! I hope she'll endure 'til the end with Harry & Co.! Cheers!
top_rant.title: A MUST-READ Book 2 Of The Already Addictive Series that's BIGGER. BETTER. MORE. Much, much more!

top_rant.text:
CHANGING COURSE-
''5 Life Changing Stars!!''

If you want a guy meets girl falls in love, gets married, has kids and lives happily ever after story.. This is NOT that story. When I 1st read the synopsis to the story I knew I just had to read it. I had a feeling it was going to be a different story then what I was use too. But, I wasn't prepared for what I was getting myself into. I can not begin to tell you how good this story is. There is so much different emotions going on in this. One minute I'm dying with laughter, the next I was crying.. Well, more like balling my eyes out.

Brett & Sarah meet at a bar. They fall head over heal in love with each other. Their best friends Caleb & Manda also fall in love with each other. Brett & Sarah get married. Thing's are great. Brett & Caleb are police officers. When they aren't working. The 4 of them are out hanging out and enjoying their life to the fullest. Until, the night that changed everything.

4 years later Brett & Caleb are still trying to pick up the pieces of their lives...

That's when we meet Jesse. She works at the coffee shop where the guys go and get their breakfast every morning. Jesse has such a crush on Brett. Every morning they flirt with each other like crazy. It's really cute to read. She finally gets the guts up to ask him out. Before he has the chance to say No. Caleb answers for him & he's forced to go.

When they have their 1st date Brett dropped a bomb that Jesse AND even me was not expecting. He was married!!! Except, not in the way we'd all think. Sara isn't the same woman you'd remember. She went from the happy go-lucky woman. Who's madly in love with Brett, to a shell of herself. Sarah hates Brett with everything she has. She hates herself even more. That night replays in her 24/7 & all she wants is it to end. Sarah wants Brett gone. Out of her life for good. Brett on the other hand, took his vows to heart, "In sickness & health. Till death do us part."

Jesse was shocked. When Brett saw her get upset it did something to him. It was then he realized he needed her in his life. They attempt the whole 'friend' thing. But, they both realize rather quickly that their friendship is a lot stronger. The feelings that they have for each other are not what they expected. Yet, every time they're together they ignore the tall, beautiful, blonde, crazy elephant in the room.

Brett realizes very quickly that Sarah wasn't the only one that died that terrible night 4yrs ago. They all did!! Except when he's around Jesse. He feels alive. He has starting to feel things that he hasn't felt since Sarah. Jesse knows that she is falling head over heals for Brett. But, knows that she must keep her heart guarded. Because, at the end of the day Jesse doesn't know if Brett can ever move on from Sarah. She dosen't just want to sit around and wait forever. Brett knows he must make a decision."

This is most definitely not your typical love triangle. Brett & Sarah are only married on paper. Brett & Jesse are falling harder & harder. Sarah is now on a mission. Make Jesse's life as miserable as possible. Caleb is the good guy. He wants Jesse to be safe. Wants Brett to move on. Wants Sara gone. When thing's take a turn for the worse. Brett must decide what & who he wants in his life. He needs to do what's best for everyone involved. That includes letting 1 of the 2 women he's ever loved go.

You will not be disappointed. For a debut novel Aly Martinez nailed it. I am really, really looking forward to read what she writes in the future!

STOLEN COURSE-
''5 Huge Heart Breaking Stars!!''

Aly Martinez has done it to me AGAIN!!! I was an absolute wreck from beginning to end of the book. Not that I was shocked by this. In her first book Changing Course we learned the 2 couples lives were changed forever! Brett & Caleb are police officers.Brett's wife Sarah & Caleb's fiance Manda were involved in a car accident. Sarah survived & Manda did not. We saw in Changing Course Brett moved on with Jesse. At the end of Changing Course Sarah went after Jesse. We ended it with Caleb carrying a broken Sarah out. It was there I knew I was in for it in Stolen Course. But, I was totally not prepared for what I got!!
